issue_title: Flow Logs not mapped correctly to VPCs/Subnets
Drop six 1.9 support
issue_body:
The version of `pytest` on Travis's 3.6 environment seems to rely on `six>=1.10` so our `six==1.9` test config fails. See, e.g., https://travis-ci.org/html5lib/html5lib-python/jobs/308631931

Given 1.10 is now over two years old, I'd hope we can drop support for 1.9, and fix this by just bumping our min required version to 1.10.

issue_title: Display credential pair keys along with credential names on the service page
issue_body: It can be difficult to track a credential key back to its credential. The credential pairs are encrypted, so we can't have a search index for this. issue_title: Invalid code generation if charm name contains a hyphen
issue_body:
If a layer-based charm name contains a hyphen, the code generated via `charm build` will fail upon deploy.

```python
@when_not('terms-example.installed')
def install_terms-example():
    # Do your setup here.
    set_state('terms-example.installed')
```

The hyphen in the method name should be replaced with an underscore.

issue_title: Inconsistency between raw edit and cl upload
issue_body: **Issue by [tonyyyang](https://github.com/tonyyyang)** _Tuesday Sep 15, 2015 at 02:53 GMT_ _Originally opened as https://github.com/codalab/codalab-competitions/issues/1270_ --- The following procedure may cause bundle without host worksheet 1. On website, you enter raw edit mode. 2. In command line, you upload a bundle. If you `cl ls` at this moment, you can see the bundle is successfully uploaded and appears at the bundle list. 3. You switch back to view mode. After updating according to plaintext in raw edit, the just-uploaded bundle is missing. This inconsistency may cause bundle without host worksheet. Reference bundle: https://www.codalab.org/bundles/0x8d63b4b6feb3433cae5b152623fa64e5/ Sidenote: `cl wedit` does not have this problem.

Flow added to neighbor 128.***.***.4 local-ip 37.***.***.250 local-as 12395 peer-as 12395 router-id 37.***.***.250 family-allowed in-open, neighbor 128.***.***.2 local-ip 37.***.***.250 local-as 12395 peer-as 12395 router-id 37.***.***.250 family-allowed in-open : flow destination 8.8.8.8/32 protocol =UDP extended-community rate-limit 125000
```

Output from `show flowspec afi-all`
```
AFI: IPv4
  Flow           :Dest:8.8.8.8/32,Proto:=17
    Actions      :Traffic-rate: 1000000 bps  (bgp.1)
```


Azlux

issue_title: client.build unable to fetch local images with docker 1.7.0
issue_body:
When using docker 1.7.0 client.build is unable to fetch local images and tries to download it from the internet. Tested also version 1.2.2 of docker-py and the same issue persists.

Here you can see the output of the test.

```bash
➜  devel  docker -v
Docker version 1.7.0, build 0baf609
➜  devel  pip show docker-py
---
Name: docker-py
Version: 1.2.3
Location: /usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages
Requires: requests, six, websocket-client
➜  devel  docker images
REPOSITORY          TAG                 IMAGE ID            CREATED             VIRTUAL SIZE
testing             latest              7425392ede6b        27 minutes ago      188.3 MB
ubuntu              14.04               6d4946999d4f        2 weeks ago         188.3 MB
➜  devel  python test.py 

Output of Client.images():
testing:latest
ubuntu:14.04

Output of Client.build():
Step 0 : FROM testing
Pulling repository testing
Error: image library/testing:latest not found
➜  devel  
```

and here is the simplified version of the test.py
```python
from io import BytesIO
from docker import Client

dockerfile = '''
    # Shared Volume
    FROM testing
    MAINTAINER Gary Servin, garyservin@gmail.com
    RUN echo "Hello World!"
    '''
f = BytesIO(dockerfile.encode('utf-8'))

cli = Client(base_url='unix://var/run/docker.sock')

print
print "Output of Client.images():"
for line in cli.images():
        print line # pretty printed in the shell output


print
print "Output of Client.build():"
for line in cli.build(fileobj=f, rm=True, tag='testing'):
        print line  # pretty printed in the shell output
```

issue_title: Passing host_config parameters in start() overrides the host_config that was passed in create()?
issue_body:
I had a `host_config` with `extra_hosts` defined. I used this `host_config` to create a container. When starting container, I passed extra volumes_from parameter. However, this lead to `extra_hosts` seemingly not doing their job. 

After I moved `volumes_from` from `start()` to `create_host_config` everything started working OK. 

I figured, I should report this, since I spent a couple of hours figuring this out. IMO, start() should override the necessary parts of host_config(and not the whole thing). Or raise an exception.

issue_title: Does fetch_into method does not support the acqusition using 2 channels?
issue_body:
Hello,
I am using a PXI 5922 niscope with 8MB onboard memory per channel. I want to read the continuous samples from two channels for a longer time (so only one record but very long); so I use the fetch_into method.

My code works perfectly fine for single channel for any acquisition freq  but no matter what, it does not work for 2 channels and I end up with the error saying 'The requested data has been overwritten in memory so it is no longer available for fetching.'
The code follows:

``` python
scope = niscope.Session("Dev1")
scope.configure_horizontal_timing(50000,131072,0.0,1,True)
scope.channels['0,1'].configure_vertical(5.0, coupling=niscope.VerticalCoupling.DC,enabled=True)
scope.allow_more_records_than_memory=True
scope.initiate()
wfm=np.ndarray((4194304),dtype=np.float64)
wfm_info=scope.channels['0,1'].fetch_into(wfm,relative_to=FetchRelativeTo.READ_POINTER,offset=0,record_number=0, num_records=1,timeout=datetime.timedelta(seconds=-1.0))
scope.close()
```

The above code works perfectly fine for single channel. Is there something which I am missing..?

Thanks,
Swapnil

issue_title: Incorrect generation of nidcpower.Session. create_advanced_sequence() and SetSequence()
issue_body:
This is the NI-DCPower function:

```
ViStatus _VI_FUNC niDCPower_CreateAdvancedSequence(
      ViSession vi,
      ViConstString sequenceName,
      ViInt32 attributeIDCount,
      const ViInt32 attributeIDs[],
      ViBoolean setAsActiveSequence);

This is the signature of the corresponding Session method:

```
    def create_advanced_sequence(self, sequence_name, attribute_id_count, attribute_ids, set_as_active_sequence=True):

**attribute_id_count** should not be in the method, instead it should be derived from len(attribute_ids) and passed to the library that way. We actually request that in nimi-python/src/nidcpower/metadata/functions_addon.py yet it doesn't appear to be working.

issue_title: txaio std lib logging
issue_body:
Please see https://groups.google.com/forum/?utm_source=digest&utm_medium=email#!topic/autobahnws/vxb4e03IUOQ
``` python
# Name would allow for _TxaioLogWrapper to not call std lib logging.getLogger with no name thus defaulting to root logging instance 
def make_logger(name=None):
    logger = _TxaioLogWrapper(logging.getLogger(name))
    # remember this so we can set their levels properly once
    # start_logging is actually called
    _loggers.add(logger)
    return logger


def start_logging(name=None, out=_stdout, level='info'):
    """
    Begin logging.

    :param out: if provided, a file-like object to log to. By default, this is
                stdout.
    :param level: the maximum log-level to emit (a string)
    """
    global _log_level, _loggers, _started_logging     ****** I tried setting log level to "none" and that passes this test but isn't implemented and throws another error
    if level not in log_levels:
        raise RuntimeError(
            "Invalid log level '{0}'; valid are: {1}".format(
                level, ', '.join(log_levels)
            )
        )

    if _started_logging:
        return

    _started_logging = True
    _log_level = level

    handler = _TxaioFileHandler(out)
    logging.getLogger(name).addHandler(handler)   ****** Name here too so that root logger isn't used
    # note: Don't need to call basicConfig() or similar, because we've
    # now added at least one handler to the root logger
    logging.raiseExceptions = True  # FIXME
    level_to_stdlib = {
        'critical': logging.CRITICAL,
        'error': logging.ERROR,
        'warn': logging.WARNING,
        'info': logging.INFO,
        'debug': logging.DEBUG,
        'trace': logging.DEBUG,
    }
    logging.getLogger(name).setLevel(level_to_stdlib[level])   ****** Name here too so that root logger isn't used
    # make sure any loggers we created before now have their log-level
    # set (any created after now will get it from _log_level
    for logger in _loggers:
        logger._set_log_level(level)
```

Maybe even better we could pass a std lib logging instance into make_logger, in case we are already using the root logger instance?

issue_title: Amend README.md to recommend pip installation as the default

issue_body: Beaker isn't quite set up to handle this yet, but when they are, it'd be nice to have a flag that will let you continue training from a beaker experiment that failed due to memory or other issues.  You would pass in the dataset id as the argument to `--continue-from`, and the script would mount that as a source _and_ as the output directory, and would pass the `--continue` flag (from #770) to `python -m allennlp.run train`.

issue_title: Opentsdb connections in hung state
issue_body:
When using diamond to send to opentsdb we have found the diamond process to occasionally stop sending any metrics to opentsdb.
This does match up with the times when one or more of the opentsdb nodes attached to load balancer (AWS ELB) goes out of the cluster leaving the diamond socket in CLOSE_WAIT state.
I have tried a local patch which adds TCP keepalive and reconnection interval to get around the issue. Happy to submit it as a patch here.

issue_title: MongoDbCollector sends several same-name metrics at a time, the latest set overwrites al the data sent to Graphite
issue_body:
OS: Ubuntu 16.04, Diamond: 4.0.515

I was studying the issue of MongoDbCollector providing zero metrics for MongoDb in Graphite. Finally, I saw via catching the traffic that the MongoDB collector sends data sets like those below:

    servers.servername.mongo.network_per_sec.numRequests 0 1497791440
    servers.servername.mongo.network_per_sec.bytesOut 0 1497791440
    servers.servername.mongo.network_per_sec.bytesIn 0 1497791440
    servers.servername.mongo.network_per_sec.numRequests 0 1497791440
    servers.servername.mongo.network_per_sec.bytesOut 0 1497791440
    servers.servername.mongo.network_per_sec.bytesIn 0 1497791440

The result: zero values are stored in Graphite.

Possible solutions could be:

1. sum up the COUNTER type of metrics, use the last one for GAUGE, before sending to Graphite
2. add an option to send data to Graphite aggregator (that would require additional setup on Graphite side)

Currently, the above collector became meaningless and I perhaps will try to use the first "hack" to send actual data at a cost of wasting more RAM on Diamond side.

issue_title: Validation for URI is inconsistent with tablechema-js if field value is IP
issue_body:
Not sure which one of this is correct - is IP URI or not - but there is a difference between how `Schema` modules work in `tableschema-py` and `tablechema-js`.  For example if you try and cast `'19.16.162.96'` with `type=string` and `format=uri` in JS it works fine:

```
const {Schema} = require('tableschema')
let schema = await Schema.load({'fields': [{'name': 'ip', 'type': 'string', 'format': 'uri'}]})
schema.castRow(['19.16.162.96'])
```

But if you try and do same in python it will fail with cast error: `can't cast value "9.196.147.78" for type "string" with format "uri"`

```
from tableschema import Schema
schema = Schema({'fields': [{'name': 'ip', 'type': 'string', 'format': 'uri'}]})
schema.cast_row(['19.16.162.96'])
```

This gives me us some hard times if we use `tableschema-js` library to infer schema from row data and process the data later with python library that uses `tablschema-py` to cast values (like [datapackage-pipelines](https://github.com/frictionlessdata/datapackage-pipelines))

**Note:**  in the earlier version of `tableschema-js` they were aligned. I believe they've split after this commit https://github.com/frictionlessdata/tableschema-js/commit/fce178a113338ceb40966aae80fffe9a5633d158

issue_body:
This should be a fun and useful addition to Juriscraper and CourtListener. I've wanted to do it myself, but I just can't find time. Basically, there are a bunch of attorney general opinions here: 

https://www.justice.gov/olc/opinions-main

And Politico (of all places) has another small cache of them here:

http://www.politico.com/blogs/under-the-radar/2016/11/a-foreign-emoluments-clause-library-232027

We should get all of these and add them to the system.

issue_title: encoding of cplex_cmds in class CPLEX_CMD in solvers.py does not work for Python 3
issue_body:
A simple fix is to add the line:

cplex_cmds = cplex_cmds.encode('UTF-8')

just before line:

cplex.communicate(cplex_cmds)

This has been tested with Python 2 and 3

issue_title: CID deploy must generate full service instance env & startup command
issue_body:
It's difficult to predict CID memory footprint due to Python allocator differences among versions and OSes. Some service instances require as low as 8-16M of RAM. CID requires 24-48M depending on OS.

1. CID must generate full ENV to start application
2. CID must generate full startup command in scope of ENV
3. External provisioning should be able to use in as-is (e.g. to put into systemd unit files)

issue_title: `deis push`: push to Deis from local Docker graph
issue_body:
```
In order to deploy a privately built image to deis cluster
As a developer
I would like to execute the following commands to push and deploy a local image

`deis push <local_image_name>:<tag>`
`deis deploy <local_image_name>:<tag>` # not to be confused with `deis pull`
```

Currently as of `deis v1.0.2` all of this is possible, but through the [not so elegant] steps:

```
docker build -t <app_name>... # build your image locally
docker tag <app_name>:latest 127.0.0.1:5000/<app_name>:`git rev-parse --short HEAD` # tag your app locally
ssh -Nf -L 5000:127.0.0.1:5000 core@$REGISTRY_HOST # port-forward the deis-registry port (make sure also your docker daemon allows this insecure registry)
docker push 127.0.0.1:5000/<app_name>
killall ssh # not elegant
deis pull <core-host-docker-registry-service-ip>:5000/<app_name>:<tag> # actually deploy your image
```

This clearly not elegant (I have it running on a CI #ignant) and a few too many moving parts.

I'm hoping for a discussion around this, I am still oblivious to any related concerns around the possibility of this feature.

issue_title: new deis CLI should check for entire range of http errors
issue_body: The new go CLI code checks for some specific HTTP error codes, but should be expanded to consider everything from 200-399. See https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/master/client-go/controller/client/http.go#L104.

issue_title: CI jobs should ignore untestable files
issue_body: At least with the build trigger strategy of polling, it's possible to ignore certain paths and wildcards. This would save us significant time since the `[skip ci]` comment to the GitHub PR builder doesn't seem to work for us. Currently we trigger most jobs on any change.

issue_title: integration tests should share an app
issue_body:
Each integration test file is an island currently. In particular, we waste a lot of time creating, `git push`ing, and destroying apps, even when that functionality isn't specifically under test.

Perhaps with some ref-counting `setUp`/`tearDown` approach, we could have the tests all share a single Deis app that is only updated or destroyed when the test calls for that. This should speed up the integration tests significantly.

issue_title: Improving performance for high_comp_cost calculators
issue_body:
Would it be an idea to add [numba](https://numba.pydata.org/) as an optional dependency to improve the performance of high_comp_cost calculators, such as sample_entropy? 

Consider the following test results:
```python
# Sample of 100 elements --  ~60x faster
%timeit numba_sample_entropy(np.random.rand(100))
173 µs ± 18.5 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1 loop each)

%timeit sample_entropy(np.random.rand(100))
10.3 ms ± 140 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 100 loops each)


# Sample of 1k elements -- ~90x faster
%timeit numba_sample_entropy(np.random.rand(1000))
11.5 ms ± 185 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 100 loops each)

%timeit sample_entropy(np.random.rand(1000))
1.04 s ± 12.1 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1 loop each)


# Sample of 10k elements -- ~90x faster
%timeit numba_sample_entropy(np.random.rand(10000))
1.17 s ± 13.1 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1 loop each)

%timeit sample_entropy(np.random.rand(10000))
1min 44s ± 869 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1 loop each)
```
And here the relevant code:
```python
import numba

# Let numba jit compile the slow for loops
@numba.jit(nopython=True)
def measure_similarity(time_series, A, B, prev, curr, n):

    tolerance = 0.2 * np.std(time_series)

    for i in range(n - 1):
        nj = n - i - 1
        ts1 = time_series[i]
        for jj in range(nj):
            j = jj + i + 1
            if np.abs(time_series[j] - ts1) < tolerance:  # distance between two vectors
                curr[jj] = prev[jj] + 1
                temp_ts_length = min(1, curr[jj])
                for m in range(int(temp_ts_length)):
                    A[m] += 1
                    if j < n - 1:
                        B[m] += 1
            else:
                curr[jj] = 0
        for j in range(nj):
            prev[j] = curr[j]

    return A, B

# Define a new function that uses the jit compiled function
def numba_sample_entropy(x):

    A = np.zeros((1, 1))  # number of matches for m = [1,...,template_length - 1]
    B = np.zeros((1, 1))  # number of matches for m = [1,...,template_length]
    prev = np.zeros(x.size)
    curr = np.zeros(x.size)
    n = x.size

    A, B = measure_similarity(x, A, B, prev, curr, n)

    N = n * (n - 1) / 2
    B = np.vstack(([N], B[0]))

    # sample entropy = -1 * (log (A/B))
    similarity_ratio = A / B
    se = -1 * np.log(similarity_ratio)
    se = np.reshape(se, -1)
    return se[0]

# Old implementation
def sample_entropy(x):
    """
    Calculate and return sample entropy of x.
    .. rubric:: References
    |  [1] http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sample_Entropy
    |  [2] https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/10843903?dopt=Abstract
    :param x: the time series to calculate the feature of
    :type x: pandas.Series
    :return: the value of this feature
    :return type: float
    """
    x = np.array(x)

    sample_length = 1 # number of sequential points of the time series
    tolerance = 0.2 * np.std(x) # 0.2 is a common value for r - why?

    n = len(x)
    prev = np.zeros(n)
    curr = np.zeros(n)
    A = np.zeros((1, 1))  # number of matches for m = [1,...,template_length - 1]
    B = np.zeros((1, 1))  # number of matches for m = [1,...,template_length]

    for i in range(n - 1):
        nj = n - i - 1
        ts1 = x[i]
        for jj in range(nj):
            j = jj + i + 1
            if abs(x[j] - ts1) < tolerance:  # distance between two vectors
                curr[jj] = prev[jj] + 1
                temp_ts_length = min(sample_length, curr[jj])
                for m in range(int(temp_ts_length)):
                    A[m] += 1
                    if j < n - 1:
                        B[m] += 1
            else:
                curr[jj] = 0
        for j in range(nj):
            prev[j] = curr[j]

    N = n * (n - 1) / 2
    B = np.vstack(([N], B[0]))

    # sample entropy = -1 * (log (A/B))
    similarity_ratio = A / B
    se = -1 * np.log(similarity_ratio)
    se = np.reshape(se, -1)
    return se[0]
```

issue_title: Prevent relaying of invalid transactions (possible DDOS attacks)
issue_labels: [{'id': 815420353, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw4MTU0MjAzNTM=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/ethereum/py-evm/labels/comp:%20trinity', 'name': 'comp: trinity', 'color': 'bfd4f2', 'default': False}, {'id': 983614747, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw5ODM2MTQ3NDc=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/ethereum/py-evm/labels/type:%20feature', 'name': 'type: feature', 'color': 'eeeeee', 'default': False}, {'id': 983624322, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw5ODM2MjQzMjI=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/ethereum/py-evm/labels/type:%20security', 'name': 'type: security', 'color': 'eeeeee', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
### What is wrong?

The receipt and processing of transactions from peers is a potential DDOS vector.  A peer could spam us with bad transactions which could overload our node as *checking* the validity of a transaction isn't cheap.

This can also cause us to be disconnected from other peers if we relay these bad transactions.

### How can it be fixed

- We should check the validity of a transaction before relating it.
- We should disconnect from peers who send us too many invalid transactions.

issue_title: Figure out how we deal with disparate `Peer` classes.
issue_labels: [{'id': 815420353, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw4MTU0MjAzNTM=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/ethereum/py-evm/labels/comp:%20trinity', 'name': 'comp: trinity', 'color': 'bfd4f2', 'default': False}, {'id': 983616278, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw5ODM2MTYyNzg=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/ethereum/py-evm/labels/type:%20RFC%20/%20discussion%20/%20question', 'name': 'type: RFC / discussion / question', 'color': 'eeeeee', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
### What is wrong?

At present we have two peer classes, `LESPeer` and `ETHPeer`.  When we start acting as a light server I believe we'll then have a 3rd peer class.  Adding protocols like swarm and whisper will further expand this.  Sharding and the beacon chain are also likely to add more peer classes.

As our codebase currently stands, we *kind of* act like our peer classes are interchangeable when in fact they are not.  We've gotten this far by mirroring the API but I don't think this is sustainable.

### How can it be fixed

I don't know, but we need to establish a maintainable pattern for dealing with different peer capabilities.

issue_title: Bugs in filter.py
issue_body:
In **evaluate()** 'fd' is [assigned two times](https://github.com/epfl-lts2/pygsp/blob/70c27ee5970aa39d70f3717e989e08ac29d59369/pygsp/filters/filter.py#L157), the first seems useless:
```
fd = np.zeros(x.size)
fd = self.g[i](x)
return fd
```

********************

In **filterbank_bounds()** if 'bounds' is not provided it does:
```
if not hasattr(self.G, 'lmax'):
    self.logger.info('FILTERBANK_BOUNDS: Has to estimate lmax.')
    self.G.estimate_lmax()  

if not hasattr(self.G, 'e'):
    self.logger.info(
    'FILTERBANK_BOUNDS: Has to compute Fourier basis.')
    self.G.compute_fourier_basis()
     
rng = self.G.e
```
but **compute_fourier_basis()** in graph.py already [sets 'lmax'](https://github.com/epfl-lts2/pygsp/blob/0922f58eca5795ab57794a43d69a21eef46f4c1d/pygsp/graphs/graph.py#L536) when returning, so I think the first if block should be removed.

******************************

Hope it helps. Nice toolbox :+1:

issue_title: conda install <file.tar.bz2> doesn't work if the file has spaces in the path
issue_body: I think the problem is that `spec_pat` in common.py doesn't handle it. It should be updated to match any string that ends in `.tar` or `.tar.bz2`. Or handled better in conda/cli/install.py.

issue_title: `update` doesn't provide latest version, even when `install foo=LATEST` works
issue_body:
I tried to update the the latest version of a package (I had 0.6.0 and saw that 0.8.0 was available from a channel), but `update` just changed the channel I was using, it didn't (claim to) get the latest version:

```
(ana44clean) pandas-bokeh-scikitlearn (master) $ conda update -c anaconda-platform anaconda-project
Solving environment: done

## Package Plan ##

issue_title: Can't install rstudio from Anaconda navigator:  specifications in conflict: rstudio ==1.0.136
issue_body:
I just tried installing rstudio from Anaconda navigator but it failed due to conflicting specifications.

Here's exactly what happened:

- In Anaconda navigator I pressed the "Install" button under the rstudio icon. (Under the icon version number 1.0.136 was mentioned.)
- After a while, I got a prompt telling me to set up a new environment for Python 3.5/3.6:
![capture](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/28363225/25678603/20b824d6-2fff-11e7-8159-6917360e244d.PNG)
- I pressed "Create".
- After waiting a little longer, I got the following "Conda process error":
```
The following specifications were found to be in conflict:
- rstudio ==1.0.136
Use "conda info " to see the dependencies for each package.
```

The same thing happened when trying to install rstudio 1.0.44 via the gear icon, except that it said that `rstudio ==1.0.44` was in conflict.

I also tried installing via Anaconda prompt and got a similar error. (It doesn't look like Anaconda tried to fix the Python 2/3 problem in this case.):
```
(C:\Python27\Anaconda2) C:\Users\Joey>conda install rstudio
Fetching package metadata .............
Solving package specifications: .


UnsatisfiableError: The following specifications were found to be in conflict:
  - rstudio
Use "conda info <package>" to see the dependencies for each package.
```

for some reason on my machine the command

issue_title: Conda local install does not respect dependencies of dependencies
issue_body:
I noticed that when installing a local package conda does not install all the necessary dependencies. Specifically it does not install the dependencies of the dependencies leaving the installed local package in a broken state.

For example if I'm trying to install a local package that depends on pandas, conda will correctly install pandas but not pytz which pandas depends on. In this scenario trying to import the modules from the local package simply results in an ImportError for the missing pytz libraries. 

I noticed the previous #466 issue but the problem with dependencies of dependencies is not mentioned there. Also the #992 PR mention this behaviour in the help but I don't understand if it was a technical reason or an unplanned scenario.

Unfortunately for us it breaks the workflow of sharing local packages among colleagues.

issue_title: Conda install doesn't pick up the latest version of a package
issue_body:
We're encountering a problem where `conda` doesn't select the most recent available version of a package from a channel. All the dependencies exist on the channel (i.e., when I add the version number to `conda install` line, the install succeeds), and the versions seem to compare correctly (see below). Could this be a solver bug?

Details: I have the following on the channel:

```
[mjuric@lsst-dev ~]$ conda search lsst-sims --full-name
Fetching package metadata: ......
lsst-sims                    master_gac1855abae.0125    ac1855abae_0  http://research.majuric.org/conda/xxx/linux-64/
                             master.20150626170402.post157    ac1855abae_0  http://research.majuric.org/conda/xxx/linux-64/
                             master.20150928224422.post8    c2d9827e8b_0  http://research.majuric.org/conda/xxx/linux-64/
                             master.20150928224422.post46    c2d9827e8b_0  http://research.majuric.org/conda/xxx/linux-64/
```

Testing by comparing `conda.resolve.normalized_version()`-s of all of these confirms that `master.20150928224422.post46` is the highest version:

```
In [1]: from conda.resolve import normalized_version as nv
In [2]: a = nv('master_gac1855abae.0125')
In [3]: b = nv('master.20150626170402.post157')
In [4]: c = nv('master.20150928224422.post8')
In [5]: d = nv('master.20150928224422.post46')

In [6]: a < b, b < c, c < d
Out[6]: (True, True, True)

In [7]: a < c, b < d
Out[7]: (True, True)

In [8]: a < d
Out[8]: True

In [9]: a > b, b > c, c > d
Out[9]: (False, False, False)

In [10]: a > c, b > d
Out[10]: (False, False)

In [11]: a > d
Out[11]: False
```

However, when I run the install, this is what I get (abbreviated output; I've pasted the full output [here](https://gist.github.com/mjuric/b59eedfbf2198c07d78f)):

```
[mjuric@lsst-dev ~/public_html/conda]$ conda --debug install lsst-sims
DEBUG:conda.fetch:channel_urls=('http://research.majuric.org/conda/xxx/linux-64/', 'http://research.majuric.org/conda/xxx/noarch/', 'https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free/linux-64/', 'https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free/noarch/', 'https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/pro/linux-64/', 'https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/pro/noarch/')

...[snip]...

Package plan for installation in environment /ssd/mjuric/projects/lsst_packaging_conda/miniconda:
....
    lsst-sims:                     master.20150626170402.post157-ac1855abae_0
....

Proceed ([y]/n)?
```

. When I specify the version explicitly, it works:

```
[mjuric@lsst-dev ~]$ conda --debug install lsst-sims=master.20150928224422.post46
Fetching package metadata: ......
....
    lsst-sims:                     master.20150928224422.post46-c2d9827e8b_0
...
Proceed ([y]/n)? n
```

(so I don't think it's an issue with missing dependencies).

The channel in question is public -- see http://research.majuric.org/conda/xxx for the channel.
The full outputs of the above commands is at https://gist.github.com/mjuric/b59eedfbf2198c07d78f

PS: Do let me know if there's anything else I can try or provide you with to make debugging easier; happy to help!
PPS: Discussion of this problem begun [on issue 1919](https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/1919#issuecomment-177105531); @mcg1969 advised to open a separate issue, as it's likely this is a different problem.

issue_title: Conda 4.4.x does not prevent removal of conda dependencies from base environment?
issue_body:
Some user tried to remove `pycosat` from navigator using conda 4.4.x 
 as reported  on [this issue](https://github.com/ContinuumIO/anaconda-issues/issues/8563)

It seems conda 4.4.x is allowing user to remove conda dependencies like `pycosat` which effectively leaves them in a broken state.

issue_title: Error message when creating a new environment from a yml file
issue_body:
I'm trying to create a new environment from a yml file. However, I am getting an error message when trying to do this. I don't have a lot of programming experience and followed this website's instructions: https://conda.io/docs/user-guide/tasks/manage-environments.html

issue_title: a ffmpeg
pywin32 module after update to 4.3.27 not found
issue_body:
Today conda got updated on one of my working PCs on Win10 to 4.3.27. Anaconda was installed for the user with the path option checked. The auto_update was set to false. 

The update of all packages via conda update --all rendered several of my scripts completely useless since the win32com module could no longer be found from pywin32. The installation of Anaconda 5.0.0 after running anaconda-clean did not help. Only a downgrade to 4.3.25 rendered the module visible again. Running the script via environment did not help.

Have there been drastic changes to how conda operates? At least 4 of my machines on different windows have shown very high impact issues. All have the same installation procedure.

issue_body:
Steps to reproduce:

```
root@922395dccc83:~# sudo -u jenkins /opt/conda/bin/conda install -q -y conda


Package plan for installation in environment /opt/conda:

The following packages will be UPDATED:

    conda:   4.3.31-py36_0        --> 4.4.3-py36_0        
    pycosat: 0.6.2-py36h1a0ea17_1 --> 0.6.3-py36h0a5515d_0

root@922395dccc83:~# sudo -u jenkins /opt/conda/bin/conda install -q -y numpy
Solving environment: ...working... failed

NotWritableError: The current user does not have write permissions to a required path.
  path: /root/.conda/pkgs/urls.txt
  uid: 1014
  gid: 1014

If you feel that permissions on this path are set incorrectly, you can manually
change them by executing

  $ sudo chown 1014:1014 /root/.conda/pkgs/urls.txt

```

In older versions of Conda, it would not attempt to write to HOME when installing packages.

The end user here can easily solve the problem by passing `-H` to `sudo` (thus causing HOME to be set correctly in this case).

issue_title: # 171: broken cimport from pysam.calignmentfile (0.8.3) when cythonized under python3.x
issue_body:
Hi everyone,

I'm building a package that supports multiple versions of Python from a single code base.  If I cythonize the pyx files under python2.7, then compile the c files under python2.7, 3.3, or 3.4, everything is ok.

BUT, if I cythonize the pyx files under python3.3 or 3.4, then compile the c files under python3.3 or 3.4, I get import errors, (prob due to changes in imports under python3.x).

pysam version 0.8.3
cython version 0.23.1


The following code reproduces the error:

#### pysamtest/check.pyx
```
from pysam.calignmentfile cimport AlignedSegment

cdef class some_class:

    def __cinit__(self):
        pass
         
    def __call__(self, list reads): # not None, object seg not None):
        cdef:

issue_title: Check_Not_Unknown for entire output port
issue_body: It is quite common to just check to all signals in an output port. Would it be possible to implement a new function to have just one function to check all out signals?

issue_title: Adding NodeDef support
issue_body:
The 5.x versions of ISY now provide a "NodeDef" for all nodes, which is a way of indicating which commands a node is capable of sending and receiving. If PyISY exposed the NodeDefID and the contents of the associated NodeDef, consuming applications could understand which types of commands are valid for a given node.

In my specific use-case, this would allow Home Assistant to know which devices are which type. I plan on making door and leak sensors operate much more intuitively in Hass, for example.

(I will implement this and submit a PR)

issue_title: Major-ordering of 'delete', select-by-alias
issue_body:
As a kind of logical structuring, my organization prefixes each series name with an identifier for the owning project/group (e.g. 'common-apache-2016.02.23' for the 'apache' series, which belongs to group 'common'). When we want to select with curator a specific series, we're careful to name prefixes up to and including that dash character separating the date. We've recently switched our logging over from a nearly identical naming scheme, excepting the group/series separator being an underbar ('_') character instead of a dash character.

We are fine naming operations twice (once per series, per scheme), but we're running into trouble with our recurring maintenance script that invokes 'delete': we're finding that this change violates the verb's expectation of indices' major-, lexicographic-ordering quality. For 'delete' with '--disk-space' defined, the order in which the indices are considered is crucial to operation: it determines the order of accumulation and, eventually, which indices will be deleted.

Consider the following sample that, I think, demonstrates the [part of the code](https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/v3.2.2/curator/cli/index_selection.py#L109) that we're interested in:

```
	>>> sorted( [
		'common_apache-2000.01.02',
		'common_apache-2000.01.03',
		'common_apache-2000.01.04',
		'common-apache-2000.01.05v1',
		'common-apache-2000.01.06v1'
	] )
	
	[
		'common-apache-2000.01.05v1',
		'common-apache-2000.01.06v1',
		'common_apache-2000.01.02',
		'common_apache-2000.01.03',
		'common_apache-2000.01.04'
	]
```

Here, we want to treat both indices separated by an underbar and indices separated by a dash as being part of the same series: when the newest dashed names grow to a certain size, begin deleting the oldest underbar names. However, the example (together with the code linked) should indicate that, while we can select the right subset of indices, we cannot have them considered in the correct order.

Certainly, we're responsible for our own naming and I wouldn't be surprised to learn that this is a rare case, but there are two specific details that have me considering how difficult it would be to accomodate within the script:

1. We have fully aliased, one-to-one, each old-scheme name to its corresponding new-scheme name, and in both directions. With those aliases, Kibana seems to respect this mapping and can navigate the indices between schemes without issue. If the selection criteria pertaining to names could be expanded to match an index with an alias of the same shape, then we could select for just one scheme, be returned all indices we're concerned about and their names would have the above-named ordering quality.

2. We do specify the timestring for each invocation. It looks as though that that option is currently used only to match out index names that have the right number and placement of numerals, but it should also be possible to infer from it the time unit it corresponds to and order results by that instead.

I'm not too familiar with the specifics of many-to-many aliasing in Elasticsearch and haven't spent too much time thinking about how other verbs would be affected, but thought that I should at least articulate the problem and see how the maintainers feel about it.

issue_title: Timestring %Y.%m.%d does not contain time unit weeks
issue_body:
In `elasticsearch-curator:3.4.0` following was working:

```
curator --master-only delete indices --prefix logstash --time-unit weeks --older-than 4 --timestring '%%Y.%%m.%%d'
```

In `elasticsearch-curator:3.5.1` I am getting:

```
ERROR. Timestring %Y.%m.%d does not contain time unit weeks`
```
Both versions running Python 3.5.1.

Probably related to https://github.com/elastic/curator/issues/181

issue_title: Update issuer record (--enabled flag doesn't allow setting enabled to false)
issue_body:
Update issuer record
(--enabled flag doesn't allow setting enabled to false, does allow setting enabled to true when issuer is initially created disabled)
for example: 
az keyvault certificate issuer update --issuer-name MyDigiCert --vault-name cli20vault --enabled true --password <api-key> --account-id 117808

issue_title: [DNS] Remove '--location' parameter from 'az network dns zone create'
issue_body: The 'location' for DNS zone resources is **always** "global". It should not be exposed as a parameter on `az network dns zone create`.

issue_title: _deploy_arm_template_core not working
issue_body:
We've been passing ```params=open('params.json').read()``` to ```_deploy_arm_template_core``` as parameters, however after ```_merge_parameters``` was added (https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/blob/master/src/command_modules/azure-cli-resource/azure/cli/command_modules/resource/custom.py#L159) it blows up with: 

```
json.decoder.JSONDecodeError: Expecting property name enclosed in double quotes: line 1 column 2 (char 1)
```

The reason is that in the ```_merge_parameters``` we try to iterate through the list and only then do ```json.loads``` - we should do ```json.loads``` before entering the loop.

Running `az storage blob upload` shows a `Percent complete` progress report, and it gets printed to stderr.  This is fine for interactive use, but from a script I want to hide that output (since including it in build logfiles will make the logs messy). >>> re.match('^[-\\w\\._\\(\\)]+$', u'éééé') # No match

In Python 3.x both works

Here's the relevant section of our `.pre-commit-config.yaml`:
```
-   repo: https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks.git

    hooks:
    -   id: flake8
        args:
        - --config='.flake8'

Running `flake8` (after `pip install`) directly on the files works fine.
 
Any suggestions? Thanks!

issue_title: geometry, linear algebra, realtime fwd+inv kinematics & dynamics
issue_body:
**tl;dr** These are high level python/robot control API suggestions. Feel free to close if not applicable.

A [comment in issue 1136](https://github.com/bulletphysics/bullet3/issues/1136#issuecomment-302944843) mentioned plans for generic linear algebra tools for pybullet. I know bullet makes use of Featherstone, so the Tasks libraries may be good for a numpy style python API or perhaps just design ideas:

```python
import numpy as np
import eigen3 as e
import spacevecalg as sva
import rbdyn as rbd

# Featherstone Plucker transform, much like translation + quaternion pair

X_O_foot = sva.PTransformd(sva.RotZ(np.pi)*sva.RotX(-np.pi/2.), e.Vector3d(0.5, -0.3, 0.))
X_O_CoM = sva.PTransformd(e.Vector3d(-0.2, 0.8, 0.))

# Spherical joint & Quaternion

jSph = rbd.Joint(rbd.Joint.Spherical, True, 0, 'jSph')
jointResume(jSph)
q = e.Quaterniond(np.pi/4., e.Vector3d(1., 0., 0.5).normalized())
qParam = [q.w(), q.x(), q.y(), q.z()]
print 'rotation:'
print jSph.pose(qParam).rotation()
print 'angular motion:', jSph.motion([1., 0.2, 0.5]).angular().transpose()

# Creating Rigid Bodies

b0m = 2. # 2kg
b0c = e.Vector3d(0.5, 0.5, 0.) # center of mass at [0.5, 0.5, 0.] from the body origin
b0rI = e.Matrix3d.Identity() # rotational inertia at body origin
# here the second argument is the body first moment of mass (h = m c)
b0I = sva.RBInertia(b0m, b0m*b0c, b0rI)
# first body constructor that take sva.RBInertia as firt param
b0 = rbd.Body(b0I, 0, 'b0')

b1m = 4. # 4kg
b1c = e.Vector3d(0., 0.5, 0.) # center of mass at [0., 0.5, 0.] from the body origin
b1rI = e.Matrix3d.Identity() # rotational inertia at body origin
# here the second argument is the body center of mass (not the first moment of mass)
b1 = rbd.Body(b1m, b1c, b1rI, 1, 'b1')

# note that in the latest api only string ids like 'b2'
# are used, none of the manual numerical ids seen here
b2 = rbd.Body(1., e.Vector3d.Zero(), e.Matrix3d.Identity(), 2, 'b2')
b3 = rbd.Body(1., e.Vector3d.Zero(), e.Matrix3d.Identity(), 3, 'b3')
b4 = rbd.Body(1., e.Vector3d.Zero(), e.Matrix3d.Identity(), 4, 'b4')
b5 = rbd.Body(1., e.Vector3d.Zero(), e.Matrix3d.Identity(), 5, 'b5')

# Inverse Dynamics with Gravity Comp
# assume some multibody system is defined with mb, a list of joints, etc.
mbcID = rbd.MultiBodyConfig(mb)
mbcID.zero(mb)
mbcID.q = mbc.q

rbd.forwardKinematics(mb, mbcID)
rbd.forwardVelocity(mb, mbcID)
ID = rbd.InverseDynamics(mb)
ID.inverseDynamics(mb, mbcID)

jointTorque = map(list, mbcID.jointTorque)
print 'jointTorque:', jointTorque

# jointTorque: [[], [-24.525], [4.905000000000001], [8.047710038720992e-17], [8.495709211125343], [0.0, 0.0, 0.0]]

```

 **Clean tools to define and solve for goals & constraints for any part of a robot**, such as end effectors, which are then solved for via Quadratic Programming based approaches:
 * Articular position, speed and torque limits, Collision avoidance
 * Multi-robot contact (can solve problems involving multiple robots in contact)
 * Task Target Goals (Inverse kin/dyn):
    * Link Position/Orientation, Velocity, Center of Mass (CoM) target, Momentum, Contact force target

Unfortunately I don't have a python example on hand so here is a
[C++ example of Tasks using position and orientation goals](https://github.com/ahundt/grl/blob/master/include/grl/vrep/InverseKinematicsVrepPlugin.hpp#L616)
```C++
        tasks::qp::QPSolver solver;
        
        ////////////////////////////////////////////////////
        // Set position goal of the arm
        sva::PTransformd targetWorldTransform;
        // ...snip...

        // mbs defines the robot structure
        // mbcs defines where the robot is now
        tasks::qp::PositionTask posTask(rbd_mbs_, simulatedRobotIndex, ikGroupTipName_,targetWorldTransform.translation());
        tasks::qp::SetPointTask posTaskSp(rbd_mbs_, simulatedRobotIndex, &posTask, 50., 10.);
        tasks::qp::OrientationTask oriTask(rbd_mbs_,simulatedRobotIndex, ikGroupTipName_,targetWorldTransform.rotation());
        tasks::qp::SetPointTask oriTaskSp(rbd_mbs_, simulatedRobotIndex, &oriTask, 50., 1.);
        tasks::qp::PostureTask postureTask(rbd_mbs_,simulatedRobotIndex,rbd_preferred_mbcs_[simulatedRobotIndex].q,1,0.01);

        ////////////////////////////////////
        // Set joint limits

        // ...snip...

        tasks::qp::DamperJointLimitsConstr dampJointConstr(rbd_mbs_, simulatedRobotIndex, {lBound, uBound},{lVelBound, uVelBound}, 0.125, 0.025, 1., simulationTimeStep);

        // Test add*Constraint
        dampJointConstr.addToSolver(solver);
        BOOST_VERIFY(solver.nrBoundConstraints() == 1);
        BOOST_VERIFY(solver.nrConstraints() == 1);

        solver.nrVars(rbd_mbs_, {}, {});
        solver.updateConstrSize();

        solver.addTask(&posTaskSp);
        solver.addTask(&oriTaskSp);
        solver.addTask(&postureTask);

        // ...snip...
        solver.solve(rbd_mbs_, rbd_mbcs_);
        // ...snip...
```
[Video of Robot Following Simulated Real Time Constrained Optimization Motion Task in V-REP](https://youtu.be/nxYoOChgMKg)

- Tasks Library Stack from high level to low level APIs
    - [github.com/jrl-umi3218/sva_rbdyn_tutorials](https://github.com/jrl-umi3218/sva_rbdyn_tutorials) Python tutorials
    - [Tasks](https://github.com/jrl-umi3218/Tasks) Quadratic Programming based realtime arm control with position, velocity, contact forces, etc.
    - [RBDyn](https://github.com/jrl-umi3218/RBDyn) Featherstone Rigid Body Dynamics
    - [SpaceVecAlg](https://github.com/jrl-umi3218/SpaceVecAlg) Featherstone Spatial Vector Algebra
    - [shell script to build/install the whole stack](https://github.com/ahundt/robotics_setup/blob/master/robotics_tasks.sh)

issue_title: Zero transition probability is ignored
issue_body:
Hi everyone,

It looks like that from commit f66a053e7f6b1081f000cd871b73b04ea4a62433, zero transition probability is ignored. It was actually a useful tool for disabling transitions between specific states.

It's caused by this line which assigns `0` log probability (instead of `-inf` log probability) to zero transition probability:
``` 
with np.errstate(divide='ignore'):
    _log_prob = np.where(prob > 0, np.log(prob), 0)
```

It can be solved by keeping the original value:
```
with np.errstate(divide='ignore'):
    _log_prob = np.log(prob)
```

What do you think?

Cheers,
David

issue_title: Invalid content-type header causes LookupError
issue_body:
## Long story short

I've encountered a website that sends out an invalid Content-Type header. They send `text/xml; charset=utf-8,charset=utf-8`, which apart from having two charset parameters, uses a comma as a separator instead of a semicolon, causing charset to be set to "utf-8,charset=utf-8", but that's besides the point I guess.

The point is charset has an invalid value and when `text()` is called, the decoding attempt causes a `LookupError` to be raised.

## Expected behaviour

I'm not sure, really. Is this the expected behavior? It sounds a bit ugly. Perhaps a clearer exception should be used.

## Actual behaviour

As described above, a LookupError, like this:

    ~/source/forcefeed/venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/aiohttp/client_reqrep.py in text(self, encoding, errors)
        711             encoding = self._get_encoding()
        712
    --> 713         return self._content.decode(encoding, errors=errors)
        714
        715     @asyncio.coroutine
    
    LookupError: unknown encoding: utf-8,charset=utf-8

## Steps to reproduce

    session = aiohttp.ClientSession()
    async with session.get('http://farzand.net/feed/rss') as r:
        print(await r.text())

## Your environment

I have Python 3.5.2, and use aiohttp 2.2.3.

issue_title: Response not passed to WSServerHandshakeError
issue_body:
## Long story short

As requested in [comment on issue 701](https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/issues/701#issuecomment-330762574) opening a new issue

WSServerHandshakeError does not provide the actual HTTP Response body.
This means any error handling code, logging code cannot provide the error information that the server provides in the response body with the normal Client object.

In order to work around this issue we use a client like this to get access to the response:
```python
class LoggingClientSession(aiohttp.ClientSession):
      @asyncio.coroutine
      def _request(self, method, url, **kwargs):
          self.last_response = yield from super()._request(method, url, **kwargs)
          return self.last_response
```

## Expected behaviour

Provide the complete response object as a property in the error. This way any change to the interface of the standard response is propagated directly without additional code changes.

## Actual behaviour

WSServerHandshakeError only forwards a subset of the response. See this code: https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/blob/master/aiohttp/client.py#L434

## Steps to reproduce

Connect to a websocket server that responds to an upgrade request with an error response with body.

## Your environment
not relevant.

issue_title: aiohttp client throws http errors for the following redirect
issue_body:
## Long story short

I have been fetching the front pages of millions of websites using aiohttp, and collected a large number of cases where aiohttp client's http parser throws errors for stuff that browsers appear to think is fine. Some of these are real bugs in aiohttp's parser, others might be places where browsers do not obey the standard, and aiohttp might want to be more forgiving.

Here's an initial bug to see if you'd like me to do more triage on these.

Here is a 302 redirect that seems to work fine in curl and Firefox but aiohttp's http parser pukes on it. 

## Expected behaviour

```
$ curl http://lund.se/robots.txt -D /dev/tty
HTTP/1.1 302 Object Moved
Date: Tue, 26 Dec 2017 05:47:50 GMT
Connection: Keep-Alive
Content-Length: 0
Location: https://lund.se/robots.txt

```
Note Content-Length: 0.

If I tell curl to follow the redirect:

```
$ curl -L http://lund.se/robots.txt -D /dev/tty
```

that works and I see the actual https robots.txt file. My browsers also follow this redirect.

## Actual behaviour

bug.py throws:
```
aiohttp.client_exceptions.ClientResponseError: 400, message='invalid constant string'
```

Building application with pocketsphinx

Installation

Pocketsphinx is a library that depends on another library called SphinxBase which provides common functionality across all CMUSphinx projects. To install Pocketsphinx, you need to install both Pocketsphinx and Sphinxbase. It's possible to use Pocketsphinx both in Linux, Windows, on MacOS, iPhone and Android.

First of all, download the released packages pocketsphinx and sphinxbase from project downloads, checkout them from subversion or github. For more details see download page.

Unpack them into same directory. On Windows, you will need to rename 'sphinxbase-X.Y' (where X.Y is the SphinxBase version number) to simply 'sphinxbase' to satisfy project pocketsphinx configuration.

THIS TUTORIAL DESCRIBES POCKETSPHINX 5PREALPHA, IT IS NOT GOING TO WORK ON OLDER VERSIONS

Unix-like Installation

To build pocketsphinx in a unix-like environment (such as Linux, Solaris, FreeBSD etc) you need to make sure you have the following dependencies installed: gcc, automake, autoconf, libtool, bison, swig at least version 2.0, python development package, pulseaudio development package. If you want to build without dependencies you can use proper configure options like –without-swig-python but for beginner it is recommended to install all dependencies.

You need to download both sphinxbase and pocketsphinx packages and unpack them. Please note that you can not use sphinxbase and pocketsphinx of different version, please make sure that versions are in sync. After unpack you should see the following two main folders: 

   sphinxbase-X.X
   pocketsphinx-X.x

On step one, build and install SphinxBase. Change current directory to sphinxbase folder. If you downloaded directly from the repository, you need to do this at least once to generate the configure file: 

   % ./autogen.sh

if you downloaded the release version, or ran autogen.sh at least once, then compile and install: 

   % ./configure
   % make
   % make install

The last step might require root permissions so it might be sudo make install. If you want to use fixed-point arithmetic, you must configure SphinxBase with the –enable-fixed option. You can also set installation prefix with –prefix. You can also configure with or without SWIG python support.

The sphinxbase will be installed in /usr/local/ folder by default. Not every system loads libraries from this folder automatically. To load them you need to configure the path to look for shared libaries. It can be done either in the file /etc/ld.so.conf or with exporting environment variables: 

   export LD_LIBRARY_PATH=/usr/local/lib
   export PKG_CONFIG_PATH=/usr/local/lib/pkgconfig

Then change to pocketsphinx folder and perform the same steps 

  % ./configure
  % make
  % make install

To test installation, run 'pocketsphinx_continuous -inmic yes' and check that it recognizes words you are saying to the microphone.

Windows

In MS Windows (TM), under MS Visual Studio 2010 (or newer - we test with Visual C++ 2010 Express):

  • load sphinxbase.sln located in sphinxbase directory
  • compile all the projects in SphinxBase (from sphinxbase.sln)
  • load pocketsphinx.sln in pocketsphinx directory
  • compile all the projects in PocketSphinx

MS Visual Studio will build the executables and libraries under .\bin\Release or .\bin\Debug (depending on the target you choose on MS Visual Studio). To run pocketsphinx_continuous.exe, don't forget to copy sphinxbase.dll to the bin folder. Otherwise the executable will fail to find this library. Unlike on Linux, the path to the model is not preconfigured in Windows, so you have to specify pocketsphinx_continuous where to find the model with -hmm, -lm and -dict options. Change to pocketsphinx folder and run 

     bin/Release/pocketsphinx_continuous.exe -inmic yes -hmm model/en-us/en-us -lm model/en-us/en-us.lm.bin -dict model/en-us/cmudict-en-us.dict

to recognize from microphone. To recognize from file run 

      bin/Release/pocketsphinx_continuous.exe -infile test/data/goforward.raw -hmm model/en-us/en-us -lm model/en-us/en-us.lm.bin -dict model/en-us/cmudict-en-us.dict

Pocketsphinx API Core Ideas

Pocketsphinx API is designed to ease the use of speech recognizer functionality in your applications

  1. It is much more likely to remain stable both in terms of source and binary compatibility, due to the use of abstract types.
  2. It is fully re-entrant, so there is no problem having multiple decoders in the same process.
  3. It has enabled a drastic reduction in code footprint and a modest but significant reduction in memory consumption.

Reference documentation for the new API is available at http://cmusphinx.sourceforge.net/api/pocketsphinx/

Basic Usage (hello world)

There are few key things you need to know on how to use the API:

  1. Command-line parsing is done externally (in <cmd_ln.h>)
  2. Everything takes a ps_decoder_t * as the first argument.

To illustrate the new API, we will step through a simple “hello world” example. This example is somewhat specific to Unix in the locations of files and the compilation process. We will create a C source file called hello_ps.c. To compile it (on Unix), use this command: 

gcc -o hello_ps hello_ps.c \
    -DMODELDIR=\"`pkg-config --variable=modeldir pocketsphinx`\" \
    `pkg-config --cflags --libs pocketsphinx sphinxbase`

Please note that compilation errors here mean that you didn't carefully read the tutorial and didn't follow the installation guide above. For example pocketsphinx needs to be properly installed to be available through pkg-config system. To check that pocketsphinx is installed properly, just run pkg-config –cflags –libs pocketsphinx sphinxbase from the command line and see that output looks like 

-I/usr/local/include -I/usr/local/include/sphinxbase -I/usr/local/include/pocketsphinx  
-L/usr/local/lib -lpocketsphinx -lsphinxbase -lsphinxad

Initialization

The first thing we need to do is to create a configuration object, which for historical reasons is called cmd_ln_t. Along with the general boilerplate for our C program, we will do it like this: 

#include <pocketsphinx.h>

int
main(int argc, char *argv[])
{
        ps_decoder_t *ps;
        cmd_ln_t *config;

	config = cmd_ln_init(NULL, ps_args(), TRUE,
			     "-hmm", MODELDIR "/en-us/en-us",
			     "-lm", MODELDIR "/en-us/en-us.lm.bin",
			     "-dict", MODELDIR "/en-us/cmudict-en-us.dict",
			     NULL);
        if (config == NULL)
                return 1;

        return 0;
}

The cmd_ln_init() function takes a variable number of null-terminated string arguments, followed by NULL. The first argument is any previous cmd_ln_t * which is to be updated. The second argument is an array of argument definitions - the standard set can be obtained by calling ps_args(). The third argument is a flag telling the argument parser to be “strict” - if this is TRUE, then duplicate arguments or unknown arguments will cause parsing to fail.

Note after compiling and executing the above code you will get no output, but you should see no errors either. If you get an error such as: "error while loading shared libraries: libpocketsphinx.so.3" You may want to run through the following steps:

  1. Examine your /etc/ld.so.conf file. Usually it will either contain the list of directories that the linker looks for shared libraries in or it will pull in other files from the /etc/ld.so.conf.d directory. Make sure that there is an entry that loads from the /usr/local/lib folder.
  2. If you did the above and you still get this error. Then try running sudo /sbin/ldconfig. Sometimes the linker configuration needs to be refreshed especially if you have built and installed from source.

    The MODELDIR macro is defined on the GCC command-line by using pkg-config to obtain the modeldir variable from PocketSphinx configuration. On Windows, you can simply add a preprocessor definition to the code, such as this: 

    #define MODELDIR "c:/sphinx/model"

    (replace this with wherever your models are installed). Now, to initialize the decoder, use ps_init: 

            ps = ps_init(config);
        if (ps == NULL)
                return 1;

Decoding a file stream

Because live audio input is somewhat platform-specific, we will confine ourselves to decoding audio files. The “turtle” language model recognizes a very simple “robot control” language, which recognizes phrases such as “go forward ten meters”. In fact, there is an audio file helpfully included in the PocketSphinx source code which contains this very sentence. You can find it in test/data/goforward.raw. Copy it to the current directory. If you want to create your own version of it, it needs to be a single-channel (monaural), little-endian, unheadered 16-bit signed PCM audio file sampled at 16000 Hz.

Main pocketsphinx use case is to read audio data in blocks of memory from somewhere and feed them to the decoder. To do that we first open the file and start decoding of the utterance using ps_start_utt(): 

        fseek(fh, 0, SEEK_SET);
        rv = ps_start_utt(ps);
        if (rv < 0)
                return 1;

We will then read 512 samples at a time from the file, and feed them to the decoder using ps_process_raw(): 

        int16 buf[512];
        while (!feof(fh)) {
            size_t nsamp;
            nsamp = fread(buf, 2, 512, fh);
            rv = ps_process_raw(ps, buf, nsamp, FALSE, FALSE);
        }

Then we will need to mark the end of the utterance using ps_end_utt(): 

        rv = ps_end_utt(ps);
        if (rv < 0)
                return 1;

Then we retrieve the hypothesis to get recognition result 

        hyp = ps_get_hyp(ps, &score);
        if (hyp == NULL)
                return 1;
        printf("Recognized: %s\n", hyp);

Cleaning up

To clean up, simply call ps_free() on the object that was returned by ps_init(). Free the configuration object with cmd_ln_free_r.

Code listing

#include <pocketsphinx.h>

int
main(int argc, char *argv[])
{
	ps_decoder_t *ps;
	cmd_ln_t *config;
	FILE *fh;
	char const *hyp, *uttid;
        int16 buf[512];
	int rv;
	int32 score;

	config = cmd_ln_init(NULL, ps_args(), TRUE,
			     "-hmm", MODELDIR "/en-us/en-us",
			     "-lm", MODELDIR "/en-us/en-us.lm.bin",
			     "-dict", MODELDIR "/en-us/cmudict-en-us.dict",
			     NULL);
	if (config == NULL)
		return 1;
	ps = ps_init(config);
	if (ps == NULL)
		return 1;

	fh = fopen("goforward.raw", "rb");
	if (fh == NULL)
	    return -1;
        rv = ps_start_utt(ps);
	if (rv < 0)
	    return 1;
        while (!feof(fh)) {
            size_t nsamp;
            nsamp = fread(buf, 2, 512, fh);
            rv = ps_process_raw(ps, buf, nsamp, FALSE, FALSE);
        }
        rv = ps_end_utt(ps);
	if (rv < 0)
		return 1;
	hyp = ps_get_hyp(ps, &score);
	if (hyp == NULL)
		return 1;
	printf("Recognized: %s\n", hyp);

	fclose(fh);
        ps_free(ps);
        cmd_ln_free_r(config);
	return 0;
}

Advanced Usage

For more complicated uses of the API please check the API reference.

  1. For word segmentations, the API provides an iterator object which is used to, well, iterate over the sequence of words. This iterator object is an abstract type, with some accessors provided to obtain timepoints, scores, and (most interestingly) posterior probabilities for each word.
  2. Confidence of the whole utterance can be accessed with ps_get_prob method.
  3. You can access lattice if needed
  4. You can configure multiple searches and switch between them in runtime.

Searches

Developer can configure several “search” objects with different grammars and langauge models and switch them in runtime to provide interactive experience for the user.

There are different possible search modes:

  1. keyword - efficiently looks for keyphrase and ignores other speech. allows to configure detection threshold.</li>
  2. grammar - recognizes speech according to JSGF grammar. Unlike keyphrase grammar search doesn't ignore words which are not in grammar but tries to recognize them.
  3. ngram/lm - recognizes natural speech with a language model.
  4. allphone - recognizes phonemes with a phonetic language model.

Each search has a name and can be referenced by a name, names are application-specific. The function ps_set_search allows to activate the search previously added by a name.

To add the search one needs to point to the grammar/language model describing the search. The location of the grammar is specific to the application. If only a simple recognition is required it is sufficient to add a single search or just configure the required mode with configuration options.

The exact design of a searches depends on your application. For example, you might want to listen for activation keyword first and once keyword is recognized switch to ngram search to recognize actual command. Once you recognized the command you can switch to grammar search to recognize the confirmation and then switch back to keyword listening mode to wait for another command.

issue_title: Long running jobs in the cloud

In terms of reproducible research, this is just spectacular! Often times, some next generation sequencing experiments (life sciences) will require large compute machines, resources, etc. so cloud computing is ideal. Incorporating the ability to leverage these large machines through notebooks is possible for short jobs, but not long jobs (>1 hr).

Is it possible to perform analysis as described above but "detach" from the machine instead of having to stay logged in the entire time (e.g. Amazon EC2)?

issue_title: Add a notebook cell level magic system
issue_body:
The IPython kernel has a magic system which can be used to do quite a lot of interesting things. It would be nice if that concept can be extended to the notebook.

Having the magics only in IPython has the problem that you can't use it to call things in the stack which are higher up, like creating new kernels and running code on them or building magics which work in julia and python.

I don't want line magics, only cell based ones. This could be realized by looking for `"#!% "` at the start of the cell and if found get the second word and look for a magic (=func) of that name and call it via `func(<cell object>, <notebook session>)` (notebook session would give access to the kernel manager and so on, cell would give access to both the content of the cell as to the representation to e.g. hide the inputs). It would need an extension point where extensions could add such functions (`add_magic(name, func)`).

An example magic would be `#!% run_on_kernel ir\n<lots of R code>`, which would open an R kernel (if not already existing in this session), run the code on that kernel and display the result.

Another example would be extending the display system https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/issues/652

issue_title: Deleting some custom keyboard commands in the editor doesn't result in persistent deletion
issue_body:
I've been testing keyboard command editor behaviour which involved generating lots of commands that we invalid (but were accepted by the UI). I discovered that after starting new notebook server, all the accepted invalid key bindings were resurrected even though they had been deleted during my original notebook session.

My guess is that something is assuming only valid key bindings are able to be deleted using the current machinery, we may want to ensure that we use the same machinery for validating both additions and deletions of key bindings from the persistent state. That way even we don't catch all the invalid and valid cases, at least it will be able to clean itself up appropriately.

issue_title: Environment Variables lost on update
issue_body:
Surely, we should be using secrets.

But we are not. But environment configurations should not be lost during updates

Code:
```
def update_kube(image, namespace, deployment_name, receipt):
    """
    Set image for deployment in namespace
    """
    # Force unicode to string
    image = str(image)
    namespace = str(namespace)
    deployment_name = str(deployment_name)
    print "starting deployment of {} on {}".format(deployment_name, namespace)

    cfg_token = K8sConfig(
        kubeconfig=None,
        api_host=os.getenv("K8S_API"),
        token=os.getenv("K8S_KEY"),
        namespace=namespace
    )

    deployment = K8sDeployment(config=cfg_token, name=deployment_name)
    container = K8sContainer(name=deployment_name, image=image)
    deployment.add_container(container)
    deployment.desired_replicas = 1

    try:
        deployment.create()
        print "created deployment {} on {}".format(deployment_name, namespace)
    except K8sExceptions.AlreadyExistsException:
        pass

    deployment.container_image = (deployment_name, image)

    try:
        deployment.update()
        print "updated deployment {} on {}".format(deployment_name, namespace)
    except K8sExceptions.TimedOutException:
        print "check resource limits for namespace {}".format(namespace)
        return False, receipt
    return True, receipt
```

issue_title: SGD w/ momentum
issue_body:
Hi --

I'm wondering about the implementation of SGD w/ momentum in `autograd.optimizers.sgd`:
```
velocity = mass * velocity - (1.0 - mass) * g
x = x + step_size * velocity
```

Other places I've looked have a different version of momentum -- CS231 from Stanford and the NAG paper [1] have:
```
velocity = mass * velocity - learning_rate * g
x = x + velocity
```

Does anyone have any thoughts on why these might be different?  AFAICT they are meaningfully different, so not sure which one is correct.  

Thanks
Ben

[1] http://www.cs.toronto.edu/~hinton/absps/momentum.pdf

issue_title: AttributeError 'str' object has no attribute 'items'
issue_body:
Hi,

I'm intermittently getting the following error when sending SMS messages using the Python client:

https://app.getsentry.com/share/issue/38393239372e313531303139363139/

The reason for the error appears to be the format the Python code expects back at this point (https://app.getsentry.com/share/issue/38393239372e313531303139363139/):

```python
@recipients.setter
  def recipients(self, value):
    value['items'] = [Recipient().load(r) for r in value['items']]
    self._recipients = value
```

which differs from what the API returns (I've removed the real number but will happily share privately if that helps):

```python
{
    'items': {
        '1': {
            'recipient': xxxxxxxxxx, 
            'status': 'sent', 
            'statusDatetime': '2016-08-18T21:26:06+00:00'
        }
    }, 
    'totalCount': 1, 
    'totalDeliveredCount': 0, 
    'totalDeliveryFailedCount': 0, 
    'totalSentCount': 1
}
```

So basically this line appears to expect a list of dictionaries back...

```python
value['items'] = [Recipient().load(r) for r in value['items']]
```

... but it's getting a dictionary or dictionaries with the key `1`, and the list comprehension here is simply sending through the key which isn't the dictionary expected by `load(r)`.

Since this doesn't always happen this seems most likely to be an error with the API, as in I can't see anywhere in the code where it would handle data in this structure received back from the API and for the most part this error doesn't occur when I send text messages. Also the API docs and example suggest items should be a list not a dictionary (https://developers.messagebird.com/docs/messaging#messaging-send).

A possible short-term hack I could try would be to patch the library to sniff the data type first, e.g:

```python
@recipients.setter
  def recipients(self, value):
    if isinstance(value['items'], dict):
        items = value['items']
        keys = sorted([int(k) for k in items.keys()])
        value['items'] = [Recipient().load(items[k]) for k in keys]
    else:
        value['items'] = [Recipient().load(r) for r in value['items']]
    self._recipients = value
```

...though if I'm honest I'd really prefer not to have to do this.

I'm using version 1.2.0 of your Python client library.

issue_title: Minio Storage Service
issue_body:
Minio [doesn't currently support](https://github.com/minio/minio/issues/4186) more than 1 access key, secret key pair per server. Here are their [recommendations for multi-tenancy](https://github.com/minio/minio/tree/master/docs/multi-tenancy). They suggest spinning up a separate server for each user which seems like a non starter. 

One option discussed in sprint planning (ht @larrysalibra) was to write a service that adds an auth layer to S3 compatible backends. Users could authenticate with their Blockstack ID and the service would map their ID with a folder in our object storage and proxy read and write requests properly. That would be a slightly larger engineering project than the alongside credential issuance we initially thought we would be building.

As far as running an Minio server that could be [accomplished with Kubernetes](https://docs.minio.io/docs/deploy-minio-on-kubernetes) with not too much pain. They have a [helm chart](https://github.com/kubernetes/charts/tree/master/stable/minio) that make following their best practices when deploying pretty easy. I also have experience with this whole toolchain and would feel confident troubleshooting issues here. 

Sounds like this may drop off this sprint. What do you think @kantai @shea256 @muneeb-ali @jcnelson

import the maps lib and use `get`. Using `get` from this lib will work, but only
if you've imported the map module somewhere.

issue_title: Non-homogeneousness of the module implemented by Sonnet.
issue_body:
This is probably not a defect of sonnet, however, I would like to share it to see on earth it is a potential bug or not.

Currently I implemented a multi-layer RNN model. The cell is intended of some customized one, the code is as follows:

```
import tensorflow as tf
import sonnet as snt

class Cell(snt.RNNCore):
    def __init__(self, hidden_size, name = "cell"):
        super(Cell, self).__init__(name = name)
        self._hidden_size = hidden_size
        with self._enter_variable_scope():
            self._update_lin = snt.Linear(output_size = hidden_size)
            self._reset_lin = snt.Linear(output_size = hidden_size)
            self._cell_seq = snt.Sequential([snt.Linear(output_size = hidden_size), tf.tanh])
            self._out_lin = snt.Linear(output_size = hidden_size)

    def _build(self, inputs, state):
        i_s = tf.concat([inputs, state], -1)
        reset = self._reset_lin(i_s)
        update = self._update_lin(i_s)
        cell = self._cell_seq(tf.concat([inputs, reset * state], -1))
        output = update * state + (1 - update) * cell

        return output, output

    @property
    def state_size(self):
        return tf.TensorShape([self._hidden_size])

    @property
    def output_size(self):
        return tf.TensorShape([self._hidden_size])

    def initial_state(self, batch_size):
        return tf.zeros([batch_size, self._hidden_size])

```

However, when I try to use the code to build a multi-layer RNN, the following way is work:

```
import tensorflow as tf
import sonnet as snt
from cell import Cell

class Model(snt.AbstractModule):
    def __init__(self, batch_size, num_layers = 2, hidden_size = 8, name = "model"):
        super(Model, self).__init__(name = name)
        self._batch_size = batch_size
        self._num_layers = num_layers
        self._hidden_size = hidden_size
        with self._enter_variable_scope():
            self._cell1 = Cell(8)
            self._cell2 = Cell(8)
            # self._cells = tf.nn.rnn_cell.MultiRNNCell([Cell(8)] * num_layers, state_is_tuple = True)
            self._seq = snt.Sequential([snt.Linear(output_size = 1), tf.tanh])

    def _build(self, inputs):

        init_state = self._cell1.initial_state(self._batch_size)
        outputs, _ = tf.nn.dynamic_rnn(self._cell1, inputs, initial_state = init_state)

        init_state = self._cell2.initial_state(self._batch_size)
        outputs, _ = tf.nn.dynamic_rnn(self._cell2, outputs, initial_state = init_state)

        '''
        init_state = self._cells.zero_state(self._batch_size, tf.float32)
        outputs, states = tf.nn.dynamic_rnn(self._cells, inputs, initial_state = init_state)
        '''

        outputs = tf.unstack(outputs, axis = 1)
        outputs = self._seq(outputs[-1])

        return outputs

```

But the way is not, which is believed equivalent to the above approach:

```
import tensorflow as tf
import sonnet as snt
from cell import Cell

class Model(snt.AbstractModule):
    def __init__(self, batch_size, num_layers = 2, hidden_size = 8, name = "model"):
        super(Model, self).__init__(name = name)
        self._batch_size = batch_size
        self._num_layers = num_layers
        self._hidden_size = hidden_size
        with self._enter_variable_scope():
            # self._cell1 = Cell(8)
            # self._cell2 = Cell(8)
            self._cells = tf.nn.rnn_cell.MultiRNNCell([Cell(8)] * num_layers, state_is_tuple = True)
            self._seq = snt.Sequential([snt.Linear(output_size = 1), tf.tanh])

    def _build(self, inputs):

        '''
        init_state = self._cell1.initial_state(self._batch_size)
        outputs, _ = tf.nn.dynamic_rnn(self._cell1, inputs, initial_state = init_state)

        init_state = self._cell2.initial_state(self._batch_size)
        outputs, _ = tf.nn.dynamic_rnn(self._cell2, outputs, initial_state = init_state)

        '''
        init_state = self._cells.zero_state(self._batch_size, tf.float32)
        outputs, states = tf.nn.dynamic_rnn(self._cells, inputs, initial_state = init_state)


        outputs = tf.unstack(outputs, axis = 1)
        outputs = self._seq(outputs[-1])

        return outputs

```

You can test above code using the following code snippet:

```
def test():

    import os
    os.environ["CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES"]="0"

    t = tf.constant([[[1], [2], [3], [4], [5], [6]],
                 [[2], [3], [4], [5], [6], [7]],
                 [[3], [4], [5], [6], [7], [8]],
                 [[4], [5], [6], [7], [8], [9]]], tf.float32)

    model = Model(4)
    r = model(t)

    with tf.Session() as sess:
        sess.run(tf.global_variables_initializer())
        v = sess.run(r)
        print(v)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    test()

```

I am appreciating any engineer in Deepmind can take time to look into it, many thanks.

issue_title: ANN: Splitting up dataportal
issue_body:
As discussed offline with some of you, it's become clear between teaching at the beamlines and overhauling the [top-level documentation](nsls-ii.github.io) that "dataportal" as an umbrella over databroker and datamuxer is confusing and adds no value.

From a developer point of view, it was convenient for us to lump them together while all three pieces (broker, muxer, replay) were evolving fast,. But now muxer and broker are really quite separate, and replay is retired. Broker and muxer have no dependencies on each other, and they provide very different functionality that is only sometimes needed together.

The dataportal package will live on as a skeleton of shims, so that all old user code will continue to work. (This is what IPython did when they split up the main package.)

issue_title: Turn _MultiCall into a function?
issue_body:
Now that #23 has landed it seems fit that we evaluate the [`_MultiCall`](https://github.com/pytest-dev/pluggy/blob/master/pluggy/callers.py#L58) type.

Afaict this class contains a single method `execute()` other then `__init__` (and `__repr__` but who uses it?) and that method was only necessary for the original recursive implementation.

I'm wondering can we simplify the whole thing into a simple function? 

Reasons for:
- `_MultiCall` keeps no state since it's execute method [is always called directly after instantiation](https://github.com/pytest-dev/pluggy/blob/master/pluggy/__init__.py#L233)
- `_MultiCall` instances are never *reused*
- having a function is less code, faster and arguably easier to cythonize
