issue_title: Failing Tests pass if fixtures use event loop
issue_body:
This came up when I was writing tests for some code that used `asyncpg`. 

Essentially, if a pytest fixture uses the `@pytest.mark.asyncio` decorator or just directly calls the event loop from inside a synchronous function, the async tests which require that fixture will pass regardless of their content.

I've put together a minimal example here https://github.com/cshenton/pytest-asyncio-bug that only uses the standard library and `pytest`.

There are async tests, each of which awaits `asyncio.sleep()` then calls `assert False`. They all depend on a fixture which also calls `asyncio.sleep()`, in different ways, and they all pass on my machine.

issue_title: Multilingual not working on 127.0.0.1:8000/xx-xx/catalogue/category/some-item
issue_body:
I have two languages.
In the settings.py:
`LANGUAGES = (
    ('en', gettext_noop('English')),
    ('zh-cn', gettext_noop('Simplified Chinese')),
)
`
When i go to http://127.0.0.1:8000/zh-cn/catalogue/category/some-item,
the page will redirect to http://127.0.0.1:8000/en/catalogue/category/some-item
And the status code of the request is 301 MOVED PERMANENTLY (from cache)

Everything seems working normally except the category page.
All these pages can display Chinese language.
http://127.0.0.1:8000/zh-cn/offers/
http://127.0.0.1:8000/zh-cn/catalogue/
http://127.0.0.1:8000/zh-cn/catalogue/some-item/
http://127.0.0.1:8000/zh-cn/dashboard/

issue_title: CLI-Created Web Projects All have the Same Project ID For User Secrets
issue_body:
It seems that all projects - even on different machines - are using this same GUID when created from the CLI: `0799fe3e-6eaf-4c5f-b40e-7c6bfd5dfa9a`, resulting in a shared set of user secrets. The userSecretsId for all project.json files created is `aspnet-WebApplication-0799fe3e-6eaf-4c5f-b40e-7c6bfd5dfa9a`.

These projects are created with `dotnet new -t web`.

issue_title: Urgent: Ubuntu 18.04 has moved to curl 4 isntead of 3
issue_body: Some tools such as cmake in Ubuntu 18.04 are making use of libcurl4 instead of libcurl3. These two versions may not be installed at the same time. This means that one cannot have cmake and .NET COre isntalled at the same time. Please make a repo for 18.04 targeting libcurl4.

issue_title: Visual Studio support for Gitlab Registry
issue_body: I've been toying around with using an onsite Gitlab instance to host a registry for Docker images for .NET Core 2.1 projects, but I'm having great difficulty using the publish feature in Visual Studio 15.7.3. It seems the publish feature has been designed to work with only two registries: Azure and Docker Hub, despite the "Custom" selection. It could certainly be that I'm not doing this right, or there could be an issue with Gitlab's registry implementation, but I have debugged as much as I can and have found that the custom feature lacks full support for Gitlab's registry. I should note that I can easily publish to Gitlab's registry from the command line, but not when using Visual Studio. Does anyone have any insight here?

issue_title: .NET Core 2.0 Console App and publish by SCD (win10-x64) will fail
issue_body:
# Issue Title

I use Visual Studio 2017 (15.4.1) and  just create a .NET Core 2.0 Console Project, then publish by RuntimeIdentifiers of win10-x64 and show dialog for "Publish failed" and will get  "Could not copy the file "obj\Release
etcoreapp2.0\win10-x64\ConsoleApp3.dll" because it was not found" error message. 

> Any other RuntimeIdentifiers will also publish failed.

`This result will cause me to fail to produce SCD files.`

But if I publish above SCD by CLI : `dotnet publish -c Release -r win10-x64`, it work fine and no problem.

# General

My environment is Windows 10 Build 1607 or 1709, and Visual Studio 2017 Enterprise 15.4.1.

The content of .csproj is

```XML
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">

  <PropertyGroup>
    <OutputType>Exe</OutputType>
    <TargetFramework>netcoreapp2.0</TargetFramework>
    <RuntimeIdentifiers>win10-x64</RuntimeIdentifiers>
  </PropertyGroup>

</Project>
```

Following is my console app sample code

```csharp
    class Program
    {
        static void Main(string[] args)
        {
            Console.WriteLine("Hello World!");
        }
    }
```

You can see my all operation on [this video](https://youtu.be/c9YHNQlkE28).

issue_title: Sensible default handling of lineages when the client is re-run.
issue_body:
Suppose the following events occur:

1. On Tuesday the user runs <tt>letsencyrpt</tt> on all her domains (perhaps she has 5)
2. On Wednesday she adds a new vhost
3. On Thursday she runs <tt>letsencrypt</tt> on all her names again (now she has 6)

Step 3 above shouldn't create a second certificate lineage which is renewed along with the first; by default it should create a successor to the first.  There can be a command line flag (perhaps <tt>--duplicate</tt>) to actually create a second, if that's what you really want.

Now suppose this case:

1. On Tuesday the user runs <tt>letsencrypt --vhost sni</tt> to make 5 certs
2. On Wednesday she adds a new vhost
3. On Thursday she runs <tt>letsencrypt --vhost sni</tt> again to make 6 certs

The second command should either not make new certs for the 5 existing ones, or should make successors in their lineages, unless <tt>--duplicate</tt> is present.

issue_title: Install Python2.7 via SCL on CentOS6 with letsencrypt-auto?
issue_body:
CentOS 6 ships with Python 2.6 by default, which isn't 100% stably supported by letsencrypt, and which the Python community at large is trying to drop support for. But there's good news! CentOS has support for Software Collections which make it possible to install Python 2.7 in parallel: https://www.softwarecollections.org/en/scls/rhscl/python27/

Would it make sense for `letsencrypt-auto` to just install a Python 2.7, in addition to all the other packages it provides, in order to a) ensure users always get a fully functional environment, b) help encourage folks to move to Python 2.7

issue_title: Expand the scope of letshelp-letsencrypt
issue_body:
Initially, `letshelp-letsencrypt` was created to allow users to submit their Apache configuration for testing with `letsencrypt-compatibility-test`. It would be nice if the scope of this was expanded to include the user's `letsencrypt` configuration and logs as well. This would greatly simplify debugging problems that occur for different users.

A couple of things to consider:
1. As I did with `letshelp-letsencrypt`, great care must be taken not to submit any confidential information. This is partially addressed by #525, but other sensitive items must be considered like crypto keys. We should err on the side of excluding information that might be safe than the other way around.
2. If this functionality is added, perhaps it's worth considering making this another verb for `letsencrypt`.  Something along the lines of `letsencrypt bugreport` would make it unbelievably easy for users to report any bugs they encounter. On the other hand, some users may not want this functionality installed on their machine, so perhaps it should be kept as a standalone script. There are pros and cons to both approaches, but I think this design decision should be considered.

issue_body:
You can supply a friendly from in the existing `from_email` field. For example the following are both valid:

```python
sp.transmissions.send(..., from_email='testing@example.com', ...)
```

```python
sp.transmissions.send(..., from_email='Test Email <testing@example.com>', ...)
```

The `_extract_recipients` (see [this line](https://github.com/SparkPost/python-sparkpost/blob/master/sparkpost/transmissions.py#L99)) method handles converting either of these into the proper format for the API. We just need to document it.

issue_title: cloudpickle.dumps() uses protocol 2 by default
issue_body:
I'm not sure why that is, but protocol 3 should be the default on Python 3 and can be significantly faster:
```
>>> b = b"x" * (1024**2)
>>> %timeit cloudpickle.dumps(b)
1.45 ms ± 5.3 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1000 loops each)
>>> %timeit cloudpickle.dumps(b, protocol=3)
586 µs ± 2.67 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1000 loops each)
```
Unpickling is also faster:
```
>>> d1 = cloudpickle.dumps(b)
>>> d2 = cloudpickle.dumps(b, protocol=3)
>>> %timeit pickle.loads(d1)
225 µs ± 843 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1000 loops each)
>>> %timeit pickle.loads(d2)
67.1 µs ± 348 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10000 loops each)
```

issue_title: Empty strings showing up oddly in sketch summaries
issue_body:
Observed Behavior:

The Most frequent items table printed by SArray.sketch_summary() for a string-type SArray will include an odd column name (Xn) if one of the values is an empty string. The reason is probably that it couldn’t use an empty string for a column name when unpacking the freq_count, but this is very confusing.

>>> import turicreate as tc;tc.SArray(["a", "b", ""]).sketch_summary()
+------------------+-------+----------+
|       item       | value | is exact |
+------------------+-------+----------+
|      Length      |   3   |   Yes    |
| # Missing Values |   0   |   Yes    |
| # unique values  |   3   |    No    |
+------------------+-------+----------+

Most frequent items:
+-------+---+----+---+
| value | a | X3 | b |
+-------+---+----+---+
| count | 1 | 1  | 1 |
+-------+---+----+---+

Expected Behavior:

Use some sense-making name for an empty string. Usually an empty string means something as bad as a None so it deserves special treatment. Definitely don’t output X6, X7… etc as it confuses users.

Steps To Reproduce:

 import turicreate as tc;tc.SArray(["a", "b", ""]).sketch_summary()

issue_title: optim state has no clear specifications and prone to errors
issue_body:
This is an issue for both scala and python. 
Use python as example:
```
state = {"batchSize": batch_size,
         "learningRate": lr,
         "learningRateDecay": lrd}
    #configure optimizer
    optimizer = Optimizer(
        model=model,
        training_rdd=train_rdd,
        criterion=ClassNLLCriterion(),
        end_trigger=MaxEpoch(max_epoch),
        batch_size=batch_size,
        optim_method=optim,
        state=state) 
```
Problems include:
1) User can set any (key,value) into state without receiving any warning or error message. Our program just silently ignore any illegal states. If user misspelled the name of a key, he won't know that it never takes effect (unless the state is tracked in tensorboard or somewhere). 
1) Different optim method may just enable a subset of state parameters, and some of the parameters may have override each other or take effects together. e.g. learningRateDecay and Step

Need a checking for illegal params, and docs about the params in optim method.

issue_title: lookup_linke_turbidity is slow
issue_body:
When using time series with millions of entries, ```lookup_linke_turbidity``` becomes a bottleneck.

My first suggestion is to set ```interp_turbidity=False``` by default.

My second suggestion is to to replace the fragment:

```
linke_turbidity = pd.DataFrame(time.month, index=time)
# apply monthly data
linke_turbidity = linke_turbidity.apply(lambda x: g[x[0]-1], axis=1)
```
with something like this:

```
months = time.month - 1
linke_turbidity = pd.DataFrame(g[months], index=time)
```

The interpolation could be sped up by expanding ```g``` to 366 values and then indexing by dayofyear.

PS. I know this would make a perfect issue for my first pull request, but I'm afraid I'm not ready for that step!  :)

Gromacs 5.x energy minimization not falling back to mdrun from mdrun_d
issue_body:
in `gromacs.setup.energy_minimize` the algorithm to determine which mdrun binary to use is:

```python
try:
    gromacs.mdrun_d(**mdrun_args)
except (AttributeError, OSError):
    # fall back to mdrun if no double precision binary
    ### Some warning warnings
    gromacs.mdrun(**mdrun_args)
```
This should, in practice invoke `gmx mdrun <options>` instead of `gmx mdrun_d <options>` (which should actually be `gmx_d mdrun`). I think that this worked in gromacs 4.x because the binary was just `mdrun` or `mdrun_d`. So, if `mdrun_d` was not found `mdrun` would take over. Since the binary being run is now just `gmx <subcommands>`, no errors occur.

Using a previously solvated system and topology.

issue_title: Provider extension not working as expected
issue_body:
Executing the example found [here](http://python-dependency-injector.ets-labs.org/providers/custom.html#writing-of-custom-providers), I get

`Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "injector.py", line 36, in <module>
    user1 = users_factory()
  File "src/dependency_injector/providers.pyx", line 97, in dependency_injector.providers.Provider.__call__ (src/dependency_injector/providers.c:2652)
  File "src/dependency_injector/providers.pyx", line 197, in dependency_injector.providers.Provider._provide (src/dependency_injector/providers.c:4580)
NotImplementedError`

as if the `_provide` method was not overridden. The peculiar behaviour is that if I explicitly call `users_factory._provide()` it works as expected, if I call `users_factory()` (which implies passing through the `__call__` method) it raises `NotImplemented`. For instance, in the following code, when I call `users_factory._provide()` it prints `test`, when I call `users_factory()` it raises `NotImplemented`.

```import dependency_injector.providers as providers


class User(object):
    """Example class User."""


class UsersFactory(providers.Provider):
    """Example users factory."""

    __slots__ = ('_factory',)

    def __init__(self):
        """Initializer."""
        self._factory = providers.Factory(User)
        super(UsersFactory, self).__init__()

    def _provide(self, *args, **kwargs):
        """Return provided instance.

        :param args: tuple of context positional arguments
        :type args: tuple[object]

        :param kwargs: dictionary of context keyword arguments
        :type kwargs: dict[str, object]

        :rtype: object
        """
        print('test')
        return self._factory(*args, **kwargs)


# Users factory:
users_factory = UsersFactory()

users_factory._provide()
# Creating several User objects:
user1 = users_factory()
user2 = users_factory()

# Making some asserts:
assert isinstance(user1, User)
assert isinstance(user2, User)
assert user1 is not user2
```

issue_title: Upstream features to the native Django backend
issue_body:
I'd like to try and upstream as much of this backend as possible to the native Django pylibmc backend.

From what I can tell the main features django-pylibmc offers are:
* binary mode support
  * for which https://code.djangoproject.com/ticket/15815 is filed
* username/password support
  * no django ticket yet
  * however I found https://groups.google.com/forum/#!searchin/django-developers/pylibmc/django-developers/pISp20wuu0E/tamJ1h8zCzsJ
  * to add this we also need binary mode support from what I can tell
* support for compression
  * no Django ticket filed yet afaict
* overriding the timeout handling
  * this is now redundant for the reasons mentioned in #35 / #36, so doesn't require any upstreaming work
* automatically handling of the MEMCACHE_* environment variables
  * however these are likely best suited to something like https://github.com/rdegges/django-heroku-memcacheify or https://github.com/joke2k/django-environ (the latter being possible once this is upstreamed, since it doesn't support non-native backends at present)

The main blocker for this is binary mode support, which is affected by https://github.com/lericson/pylibmc/issues/202. If we get an answer for that, I'll see if I can start filing more django tickets/opening PRs for upstreaming the above. (Or if someone else beats me to it, then great :-).)

issue_title: Bug in Bellman-Ford implementation
issue_body:
Hi,

Z3 generates invalid models for some queries in the `QF_UFIDL` logic when using the decision procedure based on Bellman-Ford. The model's interpretation of a function maps the same element of the domain to several different values. Below is the smallest example that I could find to demonstrate this behavior.

```lisp
(set-option :auto-config false)
(set-option :smt.arith.solver 1)
(set-option :model_validate true)

(declare-fun f (Int) Int)
(declare-fun g (Int) Int)

; g inverts f over [0, 4)
(assert (= 0 (g (f 0))))
(assert (= 1 (g (f 1))))
(assert (= 2 (g (f 2))))
(assert (= 3 (g (f 3))))

; Bound the range of g to [0, 4) over the domain [0, 4)
(assert (<= 0 (g 0)))
(assert (<= 0 (g 1)))
(assert (<= 0 (g 2)))
(assert (<= 0 (g 3)))
(assert (< (g 0) 4))
(assert (< (g 1) 4))
(assert (< (g 2) 4))
(assert (< (g 3) 4))

; f over [0, 2) is bounded to [2, 4)
(assert (<= 2 (f 0)))
(assert (<= 2 (f 1)))
(assert (< (f 0) 4))
(assert (< (f 1) 4))

(check-sat)
(get-model)
```

This is the output Z3 version 4.5.0 gives me:

```lisp
sat
(error "line 30 column 10: an invalid model was generated")
(model 
  (define-fun f ((x!0 Int)) Int
    (ite (= x!0 0) 3
    (ite (= x!0 1) 3
    (ite (= x!0 2) 4
    (ite (= x!0 3) 5
      3)))))
  (define-fun g ((x!0 Int)) Int
    (ite (= x!0 3) 0
    (ite (= x!0 3) 1
    (ite (= x!0 4) 2
    (ite (= x!0 5) 3
    (ite (= x!0 0) 3
    (ite (= x!0 1) 3
    (ite (= x!0 2) 3
    (ite (= x!0 3) 3
      3)))))))))
)
```

issue_title: Type Confusion of Z3_inc_ref version <= 4.7.1
issue_body: While working with Z3, I found that missing parsing value before call Z3_inc_ref which lead to memory corruption or remote code execution. Let take a look at line https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/6bff15e12efbadeb553dcc80bf1b5f5e3bd33536/src/api/python/z3/z3.py#L291 When create new Z3 object for example: ```python import z3 z3.BitVec('x') ``` the first argument is **ast** of parser which was assigned at [line](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/6bff15e12efbadeb553dcc80bf1b5f5e3bd33536/src/api/python/z3/z3.py#L289) without checking type of **ast** is a string or a number, so a malicious code can replace it with other type and passing these value as a pointer to **[Z3_inc_ref](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/c64d044e60684aa54c8c4d5cc4d81460707bc65d/src/api/api_context.cpp#L370. )** as the second parameter. wtf.py ``` import z3 z3.BoolRef(0x41414141) ``` As result function Z3_inc_ref think 0x41414141 as address of Z3_ast which lead to code execution because later they will increate value of this address by one. If attacker know address of some segment in python memory like GOT they can overwrite these table and trigger code execution. Crash output:

issue_title: Unexpected unsat when modifying constraint.
issue_body:
Running Z3 on the big formula included in file [smt_input_sat.txt](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/files/804313/smt_input_sat.txt), I observed a suspect behavior related to a specific variable and set of constraints.
Highlighted below are some constraints that are included in the end of [smt_input_sat.txt](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/files/804313/smt_input_sat.txt)
 and refer to variable **r_emit_urlcrawldeciderbolt**:

```lisp
(assert (<= (r_emit_urlcrawldeciderbolt 0) (zot-a887 0)))
(assert (<= (r_emit_urlcrawldeciderbolt 1) (zot-a887 1)))
(assert (<= (r_emit_urlcrawldeciderbolt 2) (zot-a887 2)))
(assert (<= (r_emit_urlcrawldeciderbolt 3) (zot-a887 3)))
(assert (<= (r_emit_urlcrawldeciderbolt 4) (zot-a887 4)))
(assert (<= (r_emit_urlcrawldeciderbolt 5) 1.0))
(assert (<= (r_emit_urlcrawldeciderbolt 6) (zot-a887 6)))
(assert (<= (r_emit_urlcrawldeciderbolt 7) (zot-a887 7)))
(assert (<= (r_emit_urlcrawldeciderbolt 8) (zot-a887 8)))
(assert (<= (r_emit_urlcrawldeciderbolt 9) (zot-a887 9)))
```
Z3 solves the formula, and the model returned ([output.1.txt](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/files/804312/output.1.txt)) has the following assignments for **r_emit_urlcrawldeciderbolt**:

```lisp
(define-fun r_emit_urlcrawldeciderbolt ((x!1 Int)) Int
    (ite (= x!1 0) 0
    (ite (= x!1 1) 0
    (ite (= x!1 2) 0
    (ite (= x!1 3) 1
    (ite (= x!1 4) 0
    (ite (= x!1 5) 0
    (ite (= x!1 6) 0
    (ite (= x!1 7) 1
    (ite (= x!1 8) 0
    (ite (= x!1 9) 24
      0)))))))))))
```
 
You can observe that the value of the function **r_emit_urlcrawldeciderbolt**  when **x!1=5** is **0** (which is OK with respect to `(assert (<= (r_emit_urlcrawldeciderbolt 5) 1.0))` ).
Now, if the big formula is modified just by constraining **r_emit_urlcrawldeciderbolt** to be less than or equal to 0 (as in the returned model), the result is **unsat** (full input file is [smt_input_unsat.txt](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/files/804314/smt_input_unsat.txt)).


```lisp
(assert (<= (r_emit_urlcrawldeciderbolt 0) (zot-a887 0)))
(assert (<= (r_emit_urlcrawldeciderbolt 1) (zot-a887 1)))
(assert (<= (r_emit_urlcrawldeciderbolt 2) (zot-a887 2)))
(assert (<= (r_emit_urlcrawldeciderbolt 3) (zot-a887 3)))
(assert (<= (r_emit_urlcrawldeciderbolt 4) (zot-a887 4)))
(assert (<= (r_emit_urlcrawldeciderbolt 5) 0.0)) 
(assert (<= (r_emit_urlcrawldeciderbolt 6) (zot-a887 6)))

(assert (<= (r_emit_urlcrawldeciderbolt 9) (zot-a887 9)))
```

I am not able to explain why the modified constraint leads to an unsat result, so I am wondering if this can be an issue of Z3.

Thanks in advance for your helpfulness.

issue_title: Directly assessing integration test coverage
issue_body:
Currently, the scheme for reporting integration test coverage (i.e. which containers were run) relies on a decorator applied to the test case, which registers the tested class into a global variable. This variable is inspected by test.py and used to generate a report of the containers which are missing integration tests.

This scheme does not have a way to distinguish complete from incomplete test cases and also relies on the decorator being up-to-date with the test case's code.

To see this issue, simply run:
`python test.py -iv`

- [x] Have you ensured the feature or change was not already [reported](https://github.com/NeurodataWithoutBorders/pynwb/issues/) ?
- [x] Have you included a brief and descriptive title?
- [x] Have you included a clear description of the problem you are trying to solve?
- [x] Have you included a minimal code snippet that reproduces the issue you are encountering?

issue_title: Table.append should not accept arbitrary iterables
issue_labels: [{'id': 237512667, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/data-8/datascience/labels/bug', 'name': 'bug', 'color': 'fc2929', 'default': True}, {'id': 295285021, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/data-8/datascience/labels/good%20first%20issue', 'name': 'good first issue', 'color': '207de5', 'default': False}, {'id': 237512670, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/data-8/datascience/labels/help%20wanted', 'name': 'help wanted', 'color': '159818', 'default': True}]
issue_body:
Currently, Table.append takes in an iterable that should represent a row in the table, and it maps elements of the iterable onto columns in the table to create a new row. This works well for lists or tuples, but if a string is passed in, the method puts a character of the string in each column. This can be confusing for students who don't understand this behavior. 

It would be better to check that the argument is an iterable of a type that makes sense to use as a row. Below are screenshots from a Piazza post about this issue.

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/2476063/13207294/aef17324-d8c1-11e5-8945-895256237c43.png)

issue_title: Support for redshift UNLOAD to S3
issue_body:
Parking this here, as it doesn't look like it's supported. 

Redshift will unload potentially multiple CSVs to an s3 [prefix](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/redshift/latest/dg/r_UNLOAD.html). This is supported by the [redshift dialect](http://sqlalchemy-redshift.readthedocs.io/en/latest/commands.html#sqlalchemy_redshift.commands.UnloadFromSelect) just like the CopyCommand that's used.

Happy to look into this, if you have any pointers.

I also asked [here](https://github.com/blaze/odo/issues/34#issuecomment-215593436) to connect the conversations.

issue_title: Sun-satellite angle ranges are not consistent
issue_body:
```
from pyorbital.orbital import get_observer_look
from pyorbital.astronomy import get_alt_az, sun_zenith_angle
from datetime import datetime
import numpy as np

dtobj = datetime(2018, 1, 16, 14, 0)
lons, lats = np.array([-30., -27.]), np.array([0., 0.1])

sunz = sun_zenith_angle(dtobj, lons, lats)
sata, satel = get_observer_look(-75., 0., 35786023.0, dtobj, lons, lats, 0)
sunalt, suna = get_alt_az(dtobj, lons, lats)
suna = np.rad2deg(suna)

print(suna)
print(sata)

```

The above code produce the following output:
```
[ 173.60123028 -178.56645841]
[  90.          269.90995988]
```

So, the sun azimuth angles seem to be in the range of -180 to 180, but the satellite azimuth angles are in the range of 0 and 360.

I suggest that PyOrbital always return angles in degrees between -180 and 180!

issue_title: ganga misses updates of self-built packages
issue_body:
I quite often run into problems when checking out (DaVinci) packages while a ganga session is running. The running ganga session does not -after building the new packages- send the python modules and shared libraries of the new package to new grid jobs. How I usually end up in this situation is:

1. log in to lxplus in terminal (A)
2. `SetupDaVinci --build-env vVVrRR`
3. `getpack Phys/MYPACKAGE vVVrRR`
4. `cd Phys/MYPACKAGE/cmt ; cmt config ; cmt make -j8`
5. open new terminal (B)
6. in terminal (B) `SetupGanga ; lhcb-proxy-init` (password) `ganga`
7. submit a DaVinci grid job (with `masterpackage="Phys/MYPACKAGE"`)
8. job finds the configurables from MYPACKAGE but fails since I should use a more recent version of `Phys/OFFICIALPACKAGE` (new property added to a DecayTreeTupleTool)
9. go back to terminal (A) `cd ../../.. ; getpack Phys/OFFICIALPACKAGE head ; cd Phys/MYPACKAGE/cmt ; cmt br cmt config ; cmt br cmt make -j8`
10. observe, that also OFFICIALPACKAGE gets rebuilt; check that in `~/cmtuser/DaVinci_vVVrRR/InstallArea` the library for OFFICIALPACKAGE is present
11. check that in `~/cmtuser/DaVinci_vVVrRR/InstallArea/path/to/python/module/of/configurable` the new property is listed
12. go to terminal (B), create a new DaVinci job in ganga (i.e. not cloning or copying the old job)
13. submit the new job, see it fail for the same old reason

rerunning makes and configures in terminal (A) can be done in all sorts of combinations without success. the only way i found so far is to close ganga in terminal (B), close the ssh session in terminal (B), log back in to lxplus and redo steps 6 and 7.

So far i always thought that at the start of ganga the list of present *projects* gets checked (as in, i always failed in the past when doing step 5 before step 4 finished) but it took me once again hours to realise that ganga doesn't even reinvestigate the contents of InstallAreas whenever a new job object gets instantiated.

(This is using v601r18 and v600r44)

ds = LHCbDataset()

issue_title: upcoef - TypeError: No matching signature found
issue_body:
I've come across this error when trying to reconstruct data from the coefficients given by dwt2 in a project to port some matlab code to python.  An example of the data and code that I'm using is given below.

`data = np.array([[ 1.47728539,  1.59481049,  1.59481049,  1.46021461,  1.44889259],
                            [ 1.48014069,  1.48872185,  1.67795181,  1.38459396,  1.32822609],
                            [ 1.41849327,  1.57595444,  1.50884819,  1.44937897,  1.57595444],
                            [ 1.46079826,  1.53956032,  1.54245186,  1.53089523,  1.49732971],
                            [ 1.49732971,  1.4323349 ,  1.44937897,  1.49158859,  1.48872185]])`

`ca, (_, _, _) = pywt.dwt2(data, 'db1')`

`reconstructed = pywt.upcoef('a', ca, 'db1')`

`---------------------------------------------------------------------------
TypeError                                 Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-340-79d9bbb59e6c> in <module>()
----> 1 pywt.upcoef('a', ca, 'db1')

/usr/local/lib/python3.4/dist-packages/pywt/_dwt.py in upcoef(part, coeffs, wavelet, level, take)
    341     if part not in 'ad':
    342         raise ValueError("Argument 1 must be 'a' or 'd', not '%s'." % part)
--> 343     return np.asarray(_upcoef(part == 'a', coeffs, wavelet, level, take))

/usr/local/lib/python3.4/dist-packages/pywt/_extensions/_dwt.cpython-34m.so in pywt._extensions._dwt.__pyx_fused_cpdef (pywt/_extensions/_dwt.c:7860)()

TypeError: No matching signature found`

However, using idwt2 works and gives approximately the same answers compared to the matlab code that uses the native upcoef2 function.

`coeffs = (ca, (None, None, None))`

`reconstructed = pywt.idwt2(coeffs, 'db1')`

I think that this kind of TypeError is usually raised when there is a conflict between type definitions, but everything is of type numpy.ndarray and dtype float64.

issue_title: `pywt.swt_max_level` silently returns unusually low number of levels for non-dyadic series
issue_body:
If I try to pass a numpy array with a dimension that is non-dyadic (isn't an even power of 2), `pywt.swt` returns a wavelet coefficient array with unusually small dimensionality. For example, if I give it an array of size 88373, `pywt.swt_max_level`(88373) gives 0, if I pass that function 88372, it gives 2, and if I pass it 131072 (the next highest power of 2 above 88373), it returns 17, which is log2(131072). If I pass in (131072 * m) for some integer m, it returns some number greater than 17.  This leads to obvious problems if I try to perform multilevel stationary wavelet noise removal because with fewer levels, there aren't any small coefficients to set to 0.

`PyWavelets` should be able to handle this issue by including a `mode` option like in `pywt.dwt` for padding, or at least offer some kind of a warning for non-dyadic series.

issue_title: WebUI logging strategy
issue_body:
The WebUI uses a Python logger to make its log. furthermore, the uWSGI also makes its own log.

As a result,we may have several log files:

 - [ ] webui-access.log, from uWSGI, that will contain the uWSGI HTTP requests
 - [ ] webui-error.log, from uWSGI, that will contain the WebUI console log
 - [ ] info.log, from WebUI, that will contain the INFO level log
 - [ ] warning.log, from WebUI, that will contain the WARNING level log
 - [ ] error.log, from WebUI, that will contain the ERROR level log

After some experiments, the 3 last configured files are not really interesting. Only both first files are pertinent. As such, I will make a default configuration with only the uWSGI log files activated.

Changing the application log level is done thanks to the `logging.json` file or an environment variable to set the application DEBUG mode.

I just tried building librealsense from source following the [prereq and build instructions](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/blob/development/doc/installation.md) and am getting the following kernel oops on plugging in a D435.

The system is an up-to-date Ubuntu 18.04 install: `Linux payens 4.15.0-23-generic #25-Ubuntu SMP Wed May 23 18:02:16 UTC 2018 x86_64 x86_64 x86_64 GNU/Linux`.

The module rebuild seemed to work fine accourding to dmesg:

```
[95071.490275] usbcore: deregistering interface driver uvcvideo
[95071.682686] media: Linux media interface: v0.10
[95071.686272] Linux video capture interface: v2.00
[95071.708179] usbcore: registered new interface driver uvcvideo
[95071.708180] USB Video Class driver (1.1.1)

[95071.929488] Linux video capture interface: v2.00
[95071.935082] usbcore: registered new interface driver uvcvideo
[95071.935083] USB Video Class driver (1.1.1)

issue_title: Unity instructions generate x86 version, Unity requires x64 version
issue_body:
Following the instructions to generate the librealsense2.sln Visual Studio project creates projects for x86. Trying to open/run the unity samples after a successful compilation doesn't work because it expects the plugins to have been built in x64. Adding an additional option of 
-DCMAKE_GENERATOR_PLATFORM=x64 to the instructions for cmake fixes the issue.

Please fix the documentation.

This is the same as issue #2031 which was closed even though the problem still exists.

issue_title: Report and skip tests that fail due to bad exception handling
issue_body:
According to the [Exception Handling](https://pulp.readthedocs.org/en/latest/dev-guide/conventions/exceptions.html) section in Pulp's documentation:

> In the event of a failure (non-200 status code), the returned body will be a JSON document describing the error. […] The document will contain the following:
>
> * http_status
> * href
> * error_message
> * exception
> * traceback
> * error

Unfortunately, this isn't actually the case. One discrepancy is that an `_href` key is returned instead of an `href` key, as described in [Pulp Issue #1310](https://pulp.plan.io/issues/1310). This means that the updated list of promised keys is as follows:

* _href
* error
* error_message
* exception
* http_status
* traceback

But this still doesn't match what Pulp actually does. Five tests are currently failing with messages like this:

    AssertionError: Items in the first set but not the second:
    'resource_id'
    'http_request_method'

And this:

    AssertionError: Items in the first set but not the second:
    'property_names'
    'http_request_method'

And this:

    AssertionError: Items in the first set but not the second:
    'http_request_method'
    Items in the second set but not the first:
    'error'

…et cetera.

Let's fix these failures by doing the following:

1. File a Pulp issue for each of these failures, either individually or as a group.
2. Skip the failing tests by referencing the bug(s) filed. For example, take this test from `pulp_smash.tests.platform.api_v2.test_login.LoginFailureTestCase`:

```python
def test_body(self):
    """Assert that the response is valid JSON and has correct keys."""
    self.assertEqual(frozenset(self.response.json().keys()), ERROR_KEYS)
```

And change it to this:

```python
def test_body(self):
    """Assert that the response is valid JSON and has correct keys."""
    if bug_is_untestable(12345):
        self.skipTest('https://pulp.plan.io/issues/12345')
    self.assertEqual(frozenset(self.response.json().keys()), ERROR_KEYS)
```

issue_title: Missing SQL queries
issue_body:
While recently trying to diagnose a slow admin page, I noticed that certain queries were missing from the SQL panel. By looking at the `django.db.backends` log I can see that the missing queries are exactly those that are using named cursors (`DECLARE ... NO SCROLL CURSOR WITH HOLD FOR SELECT `) instead of regular `SELECT` statements. I believe that these are server-side cursors used with PostgreSQL for code that uses `queryset.iterator()` (such as [`ModelChoiceField`](https://github.com/django/django/blob/a38ae914d89809aed6d79337b74a8b31b6d3849a/django/forms/models.py#L1136)).

So my guess is that DJDT is not recording queries that use these cursors, but I'm not sure.

issue_title: nrf52480: Calling `nrf_drv_radio802154_fsm_sleep() breaks usb-cdc-uart transmit
issue_body:
I am currently working on bringing up a nRF52480-based NCP that uses its native USB capabilities to communicate with the application/host processor, using [USB-CDC-ACM UART implementation](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/examples/platforms/nrf52840/usb-cdc-uart.c) (documented [here](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/tree/master/examples/platforms/nrf52840#native-usb-support)).

At first it seemed like it was working fairly well, but then I noticed that the NCP would become unresponsive after receiving certain commands. Depending on the command there were a few different reasons for this, but this bug is about the freeze that would happen after doing something like setting `PROP_NET_STACK_UP` to false.

When I would set `PROP_NET_STACK_UP` to false, I would no longer receive any output on the USB-CDC-ACM port. However, I know that the commands I was sending it were being received and processed.

Looking into the issue further made me realize that `ncp_uart.cpp` had called `otPlatUartSend()` to send the host the next frame, but `otPlatUartSendDone()` was not being called. Digging into this further, I determined that one of the results of setting `PROP_NET_STACK_UP` to false is a call to `otPlatRadioSleep()`. When I modified this function to return without doing anything, setting `PROP_NET_STACK_UP` to false would succeed.

Digging even further, I narrowed down the problem to the call to `nrf_drv_radio802154_fsm_sleep()` (Called from `nrf_drv_radio802154_request_sleep()`, which is called from `nrf_drv_radio802154_sleep()`, which is called from `otPlatRadioSleep()`). Calling `nrf_drv_radio802154_fsm_sleep()` somehow seems to quite reliably prevent future calls to `app_usbd_cdc_acm_write()` from triggering `APP_USBD_CDC_ACM_USER_EVT_TX_DONE`. It does not, however, prevent `APP_USBD_CDC_ACM_USER_EVT_RX_DONE` from being triggered, as even in this state the NCP continues to process inbound frames.

issue_title: Develop numpy style docstrings for pmag.py, pmagplotlib.py and ipmag.py functions
issue_body:
The goal is to develop numpy style docstrings for functions throughout the project. Following the numpy style will allow for automatic generation of html documentation for the functions (using sphinx) which can be integrated into the cookbook. The numpy documentation style is laid out here: https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/master/doc/HOWTO_DOCUMENT.rst.txt. The automatically generated online documentation for a function/docstring following this format looks like this: https://docs.scipy.org/doc/numpy/reference/generated/numpy.random.uniform.html#numpy.random.uniform

An example of such a docstring is this one for ipmag.fisher_mean. I have tested this with sphinx and it generates pretty nice output akin to that in the example numpy function above.

```
def fisher_mean(dec=None, inc=None, di_block=None):
    """
    Calculates the Fisher mean and associated parameters from either a list of
    declination values and a separate list of inclination values or from a
    di_block (a nested list a nested list of [dec,inc,1.0]). Returns a
    dictionary with the Fisher mean and statistical parameters.

    Parameters
    ----------
    dec : list of declinations or longitudes
    inc : list of inclinations or latitudes
    di_block : a nested list of [dec,inc,1.0]
        A di_block can be provided instead of dec, inc lists in which case it
        will be used. Either dec, inc lists or a di_block need to be provided.

    Returns
    -------
    fisher_mean : dictionary containing the Fisher mean parameters

    Examples
    --------
    Use lists of declination and inclination to calculate a Fisher mean:

    >>> ipmag.fisher_mean(dec=[140,127,142,136],inc=[21,23,19,22])

    Use a di_block to calculate a Fisher mean:

    >>> ipmag.fisher_mean(di_block=[[140,21],[127,23],[142,19],[136,22]])
    """
```

issue_title: Numpy interface can fail with non-writeable arrays.
issue_body:
The following code fails if it is called before a plan is cached:

```python
import numpy as np
from pyfftw.interfaces.numpy_fft import fat
y = np.zeros(4) + 1j  # Needs to be complex for failure
y.flags.writeable = False
fft(y)
```

```
In [5]: fft(y)
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
ValueError                                Traceback (most recent call last)
...
.../pyfftw/interfaces/_utils.pyc in _Xfftn(a, s, axes, overwrite_input, planner_effort, threads, auto_align_input, auto_contiguous, calling_func)
     81         a_copy = a.copy()
     82 
---> 83         FFTW_object = getattr(builders, calling_func)(*args)
     84 
     85         # Only copy if the input array is what was actually used
...
.../pyfftw/builders/_utils.pyc in _Xfftn(a, s, axes, overwrite_input, planner_effort, threads, auto_align_input, auto_contiguous, avoid_copy, inverse, real)
    171         if not avoid_copy:
    172             # Copy the data back into the (likely) destroyed array
--> 173             FFTW_object.get_input_array()[:] = a_copy
    174 
    175     return FFTW_object

ValueError: assignment destination is read-only
```

The problem is that the plan is made using the original array, which in this case is not writeable.  Since a copy is made, why not use it for planning instead since the copy will always be writeable?

The minimal change would be to set and restore the writeable flag:

```patch
82a83,85
>         # Input array must be writeable...
>         writeable, a.flags.writeable = a.flags.writeable, True
> 
89a93,95
>         # Restore writeable flag
>         a.flags.writeable = writeable
> 
```

Using the copy instead seems to work but there may be subtleties I am not aware of.

```patch
24c24
< 
---
> import numpy as np
81c81
<         a_copy = a.copy()
---
>         args = (np.empty_like(a),) + args[1:]
85,89d84
<         # Only copy if the input array is what was actually used
<         # (otherwise it shouldn't be overwritten)
<         if FFTW_object.get_input_array() is a:
<             a[:] = a_copy
< 
93c88
<         output_array = FFTW_object()
---
>         output_array = FFTW_object(input_array=a) 
```

issue_title: Support date format hints
issue_body:
Excel will output CSVs with dates in the user's locale, so a British user will see dates in the DD/MM/YYYY format, whilst an American user would see MM/DD/YYYY.

Currently it appears that csvkit (although I've only really been using csvsql) will always try MM/DD/YYYY first, and if that fails to parse, then it will fall back to DD/MM/YYYY.

The problem with this approach arises when you're using a DD/MM/YYYY formatted sheet and you have ambiguity in some dates. For example:

02/01/2014
31/12/2012

This will produce dates in the database of:

2014-01-02 (incorrectly parsed as MM/DD/YYYY)
2012-12-31

So you silently end up with a mixture of correct and incorrect dates, which is not ideal!

Ideally there'd be an option one could pass to csvkit programs to specify a preference list for parsing dates. It'd be overkill to specify every format (I think), so having some abbreviations would suffice. For example:

--date-formats "dmy,mdy"

The default date parsing schema would then follow after this if the formats in the list did not match successfully.

An extension to this may be to try to infer the date format by examining all rows globally and selecting the format that successfully parses them all (of course, this is still no guarantee of success as people may _only_ have ambiguous dates present).

issue_title: Extract submenus into a new project
issue_body:
The submenu feature is nice, but it's very complex. What if we extracted the submenu code into a new GitHub project at https://github.com/python-cmd2/cmd2-submenus, and packaged it in pypi as a separate wheel, something like cmd2-submenus. This would enable:

1. Easier maintenance of the core cmd2 module
2. Version dependency between releases of cmd2 and cmd2-submenus. If cmd2-submenus lags behind cmd2 features, users of both modules can make a decision whether they want to most current cmd2 capabilities, or submenu support.
3. Set a pattern for extensibility of cmd2 via additional packages instead of changes to the core.

I'm willing to take a crack at the extraction of the submenu code if there is interest in pursuing this proposal.

issue_title: Delete old backups when --max-log-backups is provided
issue_body:
Originally reported at https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/certbot-max-log-backups-not-working-as-expected/53090, if you have more than X logfiles and run certbot with `--max-log-backups Y` where Y is less than X, Certbot won't delete older logfiles and just manages manages the ones whose suffix is <= Y. We should delete the older logfiles.

We'd accept a well written PR to fix this.

issue_title: Allow callers to add information to the User Agent (without replacing it entirely)
issue_body:
(Based on IRC discussion with @nuxwin.)

Currently, there is not a good way for people re-packaging or wrapping Certbot to influence its user agent string. They may which to do this so that stats/metrics are available to boulder.

The `--user-agent` flag allows a caller to set the user agent, but that sets the string in its entirely, losing all of the information which is included by default.

For this use case, it is desirable to both the Certbot team and the party re-packaging Certbot that the custom user agent match Certbot user agent as closely as possible: this greatly simplifies the "parsing problem" and allows data for the re-packaged version to be collected along side _or_ separately from vanilla certbot.

Supporting an additional `--user-agent-comment` flag seems like an easy way to address this.

This comment, if set, could be inserted into the main (product-level) comment, following the OS information.

This argument and `--user-agent` would be mutually exclusive.

cc @pde

issue_title: Installer plugin documentation is lacking/confusing
issue_body:
Hi,

I'm trying to write an installer plugin. The IInstaller interface has lots of different functions that I seemingly need to override, but the documentation is very unclear about the larger picture. In normal use, I can only see prepare, deploy_cert, save and restart be called; however, there are lots of others, including get_all_names (which is hard for me to find without opening and parsing the actual certificates) and then lots of rollback/recovery stuff which somehow may or may not integrate with a “reverter” module. I _think_ some of the rollback stuff is called if I throw an exception from any of the others, but exactly when a checkpoint is set (I can be asked to rollback N checkpoints, so it would be good to know when I am expected to take a checkpoint) or how all of this is used is unclear.

It looks like somehow the installer interface might also be used for authentication via DVSNI. If that's a correct guess (so it means installers overlap with authenticators wrt. purpose), perhaps it should be split out from the much simpler concept of just deploying an existing certificate authenticated by another module?

Thanks!

issue_title: Improve documentation about gnupg2 verification of certbot-auto
issue_labels: [{'id': 245387015, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWwyNDUzODcwMTU=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/area:%20documentation', 'name': 'area: documentation', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}, {'id': 149596829, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWwxNDk1OTY4Mjk=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/good%20first%20issue', 'name': 'good first issue', 'color': 'c61aff', 'default': True}, {'id': 281347824, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWwyODEzNDc4MjQ=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/has%20pr', 'name': 'has pr', 'color': '0052cc', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
This is a documentation issue regarding the verification steps for checking the signature integrity of the certbot-auto script. 

In the https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/master/docs/install.rst file there is a Hint where the following instructions are given for verifying the integrity of the certbot-auto script:

```
user@server:~$ wget -N https://dl.eff.org/certbot-auto.asc
user@server:~$ gpg2 --recv-key A2CFB51FA275A7286234E7B24D17C995CD9775F2
user@server:~$ gpg2 --trusted-key 4D17C995CD9775F2 --verify certbot-auto.asc certbot-auto
```

If the user does not have gpg2 installed the instructions will fail, which is fine, we can assume that the user can figure out how to install gpg2 (e.g. `apt-get install gnupg2` tested on Ubuntu 14.04). However after installing gpg2 the second line will fail as follows:

```
user@server:~$ gpg2 --recv-key A2CFB51FA275A7286234E7B24D17C995CD9775F2
gpg: directory `/user/.gnupg' created
gpg: new configuration file `/user/.gnupg/gpg.conf' created
gpg: WARNING: options in `/user/.gnupg/gpg.conf' are not yet active during this run
gpg: keyring `/user/.gnupg/secring.gpg' created
gpg: keyring `/user/.gnupg/pubring.gpg' created
gpg: no keyserver known (use option --keyserver)
gpg: keyserver receive failed: Syntax error in URI
```

It would be useful to add an instruction for setting an appropriate key server to prevent confusion. For example (following the official gnupg FAQ https://www.gnupg.org/faq/gnupg-faq.html#define_keyserver)

```
user@server:~$ gpg2 --keyserver pool.sks-keyservers.net --recv-key A2CFB51FA275A7286234E7B24D17C995CD9775F2
```

Adding an example of a successful verification would also be helpful, e.g.

```
user@server:~$ gpg2 --trusted-key 4D17C995CD9775F2 --verify certbot-auto.asc /usr/local/sbin/certbot-auto
gpg: Signature made Wed 07 Mar 2018 06:23:57 PM UTC using RSA key ID CD9775F2
gpg: key CD9775F2 marked as ultimately trusted
gpg: checking the trustdb
gpg: 3 marginal(s) needed, 1 complete(s) needed, PGP trust model
gpg: depth: 0  valid:   1  signed:   0  trust: 0-, 0q, 0n, 0m, 0f, 1u
gpg: next trustdb check due at 2027-11-22
gpg: Good signature from "Let's Encrypt Client Team <letsencrypt-client@eff.org>"
```

This is admittedly a minor issue, but extra clarity when dealing with trust and verification is always useful.

issue_title: [Feature] Multiboard support in apio
issue_body:
Currently (apio version 0.1.0.2) only supports boards based on lattice ice40hx 1k and tq144  (ex. Icezum Alhambra, icestick).  For supporting other boards, like nandland go, some flags should be passed to apio when synthesizing.

We should think about how to include this multiboard support.  The approach can be similar to apio: use a command for creating a project which includes a project.json file that is read by apio for synthetizing to the target board

Ex:

apio project icezum

It will generate the project.json file in the current directory (aditionally some template files can also be added, like a icezum.pcf constraint file for example)

The algorithm for building could be something like:
$ apio build

1) If SConstruct file exist: Use it for building. Exit
2) Look for the project.json file in the current dir
3) If project.json does not exist 
  2.1 use the flags for the default board (icezum for example)
  2.2 Build it and exit
4) Read the flags in the project.json:  Size (1k, 8k), Pack (tq144, ct256...), family (iCE40), subfamily (HX, LP)
5) Pass this flags to the default SConstruct file (etither by command or by environmental variable)
6) Build

Task to be done:

1) Define the flags needed
2) Create a new SConstruct file that implements this flags
3) Define the project.json file (or board.json or whatever we call it)
4) Implement the command apio project (or whatever name we use for it). It just generates the platform.json file
5) Implement the new apio builld command

I will learn more about the flags needed. Initially I will create a generic makefile and integrate it into apio as an example:  apio examples -f makefile). This is just a legacy example. It will let us compile easily examples that have already been created (like the ones in the verilog tutorials). Then I will create a generic SConstruct file, compatible with the current one, with the flags defined in the begining.  Using apio init this file can be obtained and the user can set manually the building flags. Once it is working, we can integrate it with the new building system

In the phase when the LLCP client waits for CC or DM PDU, broken link causes closing of sockets. This leads to notifying receiver thread in class TransmissionControllObject that tries thereafter to pop data from empty receiving queue:

### What was wrong?

I'm a bit in love with the "builder" concept so take this with a grain of salt.  It may not be a good idea.


### How can it be fixed?

Tools for functional compisition based manifest building.

```python
manifest = build_manifest(
    setup_manifest(),  # stubs out some initial data to seed the manifest.
    set_meta(author=...),  # injects the `meta` information into the manifest.
    set_meta(license=...),
    set_contract_types(
        ERC20Token=extract_contract_type('ERC20Token', solc_compiler_output),
        owned=extract_contract_type('owned', solc_compiler_output),
    ),
    set_deployments(
        ...
    ),
    prettify(),
)
```

Rather than try to build something that is infinitely flexible, build a lot of small tools that are designed to be chained together to build a manifest...  We could pair on this.

issue_title: Refactoring - Invert/Rename RaidenAPI and Raiden class
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

The current Raiden architecture of the Raiden God Object having a Raiden API class inside it and being called by yet another API (the REST API), is quite convoluted and confusing. After our discussions in the Andalucia Retreat we came up with the following changes to the architecture.



## Tasklist

- Move what is now the `RaidenAPI `class outside of the the Raiden God object and rename it to Raiden.
- What is now the Raiden God Object will be renamed to `RaidenCore`.
- The new Raiden object (internal API) will only be accepting binary and each interface, like the REST API or the CLI will have to take care of encoding on their own.

This is what the architecture will look like after the refactoring is done, but before restartable tasks are merged. 
![img_20170323_115956](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1658405/24245676/17f6d596-0fc4-11e7-9316-4e2ce09fd878.jpg)

issue_title: Protocol: Don't resend acknowledged messages
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

Raiden has a hard requirement that every mediated message must use a unique non-predictable secret for the transfer at hand, this implies that hashlocks are unique and so are the messages `MediatedTransfer`, `RefundTransfer`, `SecretRequests`, and `Secret`. Since the effect of sending any of these messages is non-revertible, adding the same message into the protocol queue is redundant an unnecessary.

With that in mind, we can use this property as a means to prevent amplification at the protocol layer. For the current HEAD commit e55b2dc917ce389fed773adacc04f61efc5ca6fe, everytime a [Secret message is received](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/e55b2dc917ce389fed773adacc04f61efc5ca6fe/raiden/raiden_service.py#L692-L710) the secret is registered and [SecretReveal](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/e55b2dc917ce389fed773adacc04f61efc5ca6fe/raiden/tokenmanager.py#L159-L181) are sent for each channel that hashlock registered.

## Tasklist

- [ ] List messages types that are unique (e.g. Ping / Pong are not)
- [ ] Update the protocol layer to ignore the same message:

```
add_message(M):
  if M is of a type that is unique: 
    if M in queue: ignore
    if M was sent and acknowledged: ignore
```

issue_title: Django ImageField is a SubFactory now, should it be?
issue_body:
I've received an issue https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest-factoryboy/issues/58

pytest-factoryboy introspects the declarations in order to create fixtures automatically related to the current factory.

So far I'm comparing isinstance(decl, SubFactory) and isinstance(decl, RelatedFactory).
Now this breaks for the django.ImageField since it is a subclass of a Dict and also a SubFactory (really weird hierarchy). Is it not abused? Should it really be a SubFactory?
If ImageField is a SubFactory why doesn't it accept a factory as a parameter in order to respect the interface of the SubFactory (common OOP sense). If ImageField IS A SubFactory == False then why it won't use composition and invoke a dict factory inside the implementation?

I could check exact types like if type(decl, SubFactory) then create fixture for the model referenced by SubFactory. But this will prevent from subclassing a SubFactory in the valid cases.

Any suggestions?

pos         Seattle is good        {"wIdx": 0, "features" : {"city": 0.7}}

In the model I have an embedding table for the dict_features that I am learning its weights as part of the model. 

I am wondering what is the best way to implement this field, specifically, what the preprocessing/postprocessing/numericalize functions should look like?

issue_title: Multi30k dataset splits method missing argument
issue_body:
TL;DR
 `Multi30k.splits()` is missing argument `path`

I encountered a problem when loading Multi30k machine translation dataset. I have first donwloaded it to see the extensions needed and to go through data a bit before creating dataset. When I tried to create the dataset, I got `
FileNotFoundError: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: 'data/Multi30k/multi30k\\test.de'`... The name of test set isn't test, but test2016. Full traceback can be seen at the bottom.

Anyway, I went through the code of `Multi30k` and `TranslationDataset` and found this:
- Multi30k
```
return super(Multi30k, cls).splits(
                     exts, fields, root, train, validation, test, **kwargs)
```
- TranslationDataset
```
def splits(cls, exts, fields, path=None, root='.data',
               train='train', validation='val', test='test', **kwargs):
```

The path variable is missing, meaning the arguments are passed wrong. I assume this isn't intentional, so I think someone can start working on PR. As this is a minor issue, I would propose adding a flag to this issue encouraging beginners to submit their first PR. If anyone won't be interested in doing that, I offer myself.

Minimal working example:
```
from torchtext.datasets import Multi30k
Multi30k.download('data')
EN = Field()
DE = Field()
ds = Multi30k.splits(('.de','.en'),[('de',DE),('en',EN)],'data/multi30k')

issue_title: Adding the "train" argument back to "postprocess"
issue_body: I'd like to make a case for adding the "train" argument back to the Field's postprocess call. Fields tend to be shared across training and test sets, so the train argument was a flexible way to apply different postprocessing at training and test time. One example: using "postprocessing" to add noise to the input text dynamically. Without the train argument, I don't think there is a clean way to deactivate the noise at test time. Happy to take suggestions for work arounds, but I think having the train argument should generalize to more use cases.

issue_title: Be more explicit about how to run scripts with the autoscaler
issue_body:
The autoscaling documentation should walk the user through how exactly to set up a cluster and execute a script, and especially describe what needs to be executed where, i.e. the "ray create_or_update" command is run on a laptop and the script is run on the head node.

This may seem obvious to us, but is not obvious for users and it is really important that the experience of setting up a cluster and running a script is as smooth as possible.

This can either be part of the autoscaling documentation, or be part of a tutorial "setting up a cluster".

issue_title: Remove all required tracers from GFS
issue_body: The Fortran code for GFS appears to never actually use any of the tracers (including specific humidity), other than to assign them into its tracer arrays and advect those arrays. We should refactor the code so that all tracer array preparation occurs in Python, and the Fortran code can accept zero-sized tracer arrays. Then GFS will use only the tracers registered with Sympl. CliMT could choose to register certain tracers with Sympl.

issue_title: Ensuring non-negativity for GFS
issue_body: Should GFS actually be the component to ensure that moisture and other tracers are non-negative, or should there be a component called after GFS which does so? For example, a user might have tracers which are okay to go negative, or might want to ensure global conservation of moisture. Currently the only way to modify this behavior is to modify GFS, but instead it could be that the user swaps out different components outside of the model. We could have a flag `make_tracers_nonnegative=False` (or `True`) on initialization if you like.

issue_title: Skipping reserved words causes other passwords on the same line to be ignored
issue_body:
If a given line has two passwords and the first is a reserved word, the second will be incorrectly skipped.

e.g. in `snmp-server user a b auth md5 ipaddress priv RemoveMe`, `RemoveMe` is a password that should be anonymized and `ipaddress` is a reserved word that should be left untouched, however Netconan stops searching for additional passwords once it finds the reserved word.

issue_title: download fails with 0-byte files
Support auxiliary file transfer
issue_body:
I am developing HEP-specific decorators `cmssw_python_app` and `cmssw_bash_app`. These require the presence of the same specific files on the remote side for every app running on the remote side. It would be nice to be able to handle this automatically in the decorator like this:

```python
input = File('foo')

@cmssw_python_app
def bar(inputs=[input]):
    with open(inputs[0].filepath, 'r') as f:
        print(f.read())
```

instead of forcing the user to be responsible for it (and thus have to write the same repeated code each time), and have it pollute the `inputs` list:

```python
aux_file = File('baz')
input = File('foo')

@cmssw_python_app
def foo(inputs=[aux_file, input]):
    with open(inputs[1].filepath, 'r') as f:
        print(f.read())

```

One way to accomplish this would be to add an `auxiliary_files` keyword argument to the `app` decorator. This list could be propagated to `DataFlowKernel.submit` such that `file.stage_in` is called for each file in the `auxiliary_files` list as well as each file in the `inputs` list. In that way we could re-use all of machinery for staging files (and future caching optimizations, etc). This could be useful in other cases where you always need the same file transferred for a given app.

I want Studio to serve a model over an http endpoint similar to TensorFlow serving.  Here are the details

1.  Support the same frameworks we support for model building, Keras, TF, Pytorch, etc.

2.  "studio serve ..." command should spin up a compute instance with the specified compute, copy the model weights and the python serving code and start up a flask service on that instance with a prediction endpoint

3.  User may need some way to specific details about the parameters that the endpoint will expect such as a json file with base64 encoded images, json/CSV with tabular data, etc. 

4.  We don't want the compute instance to stay up indefinitely so user can specify a keepalive period (1hr, 1day, 1month, forever).  Default should be 1 hour. 

5. Flask serving logs should be managed by studio and audible (access logs and errors)

issue_title: Validate AST and Specify API
issue_body:
I think it would be good to start the AST API discussion. I'm finding myself expanding array expressions for the xml backend which I know has already been done for the casadi backend. It would be nice if this was handeled on the AST side.

If we look at the ModelicaXML tree descriptions for a guide. I think it would make sense to be able to ask  the pymoca API for the tree at any stage 1, 2, 3 or 4. This would let us share the array expansion work between backends, which is my current motivation.

> 1. Modelica source code. This format is an abstract representation of Modelica code and should provide a one to one mapping between Modelica source code and a corresponding XML representation.
> 2. Instantiated hierarchical models. In this model representation, Modelica modifiers have taken effect, and the hierarchical structure is preserved.
> 3. Flattened Modelica models. This model representation is flat in the sense that it does not contain hierarchical model elements. It may, however, contain array declarations.
> 4. Flattened transformed models. In this representation, array variables and equations have been scalarized, alias variables have been eliminated and DAE index has been reduced, if needed.

Maybe something like this?
```python
# step 1
ast_code = parse(pymola)
generate_xml_code(ast_code).validate()

# step 2
ast_inst = instantiate(ast_code)
generate_xml_inst(ast_int).validate()

# step 3
ast_flat = flatten(ast_inst)
generate_xml_flat(ast_flat).validate()

# step 4
ast_reduced = reduce(ast_flat)
generate_xml_reduced(ast_reduced).validate()

# send to backends
generate_casadi(ast_flat... or ast_reduced, whatever is easiest)
```

issue_title: DeprecationWarning: 'async' and 'await' will become reserved keywords in Python 3.7
issue_body:
Since there are few places that `async` keyword is used, we get this warning, which means RQ will break on 3.7.

Here is what PEP8 advised to do in these cases:

> single_trailing_underscore_: used by convention to avoid conflicts with Python keyword, e.g.
> 
> Tkinter.Toplevel(master, class_='ClassName')

I would open a PR to change it to `async_`  but this will be breaking change, what do you guys think?

issue_title: No matching overloads found for java.o.File
issue_body:
Hi all,

I have a few classes defined in java all packaged into a jar file.
For the purposes of simplicity lets say the classes are called classA and classB.

classA has a method "public classB load(java.o.File file)", i.e. it has a public method that takes a java.io.File object and returns an object of type classB.

I have the started the JVM with class path set to point to the jar file and am able to call methods from the classes defined in the jar.
However when I try to call method load(...) described above from classA, I get a runtime error saying:
"RuntimeError: No matching overloads found. at native/common/jp_method.cpp:121"

The method is valid and runs when used directly from java. In python2.7, I can observe the matchReport() output for the "load" method and it gives an output:

'Match report for method load, has 1 overloads.\n  Lnet/sf/project/classB; (Ljava/io/File;) ==> NONE\n'

Does anyone know what I might be doing wrong. I am using the v0.6.1 of jpype with python2.7 on Mac OSx Yosemite (10.10.5).

Best,
Sanket

issue_title: Use another approach to testing MySQL connectivity
issue_body:
JohnD...@gmail.com, 2010-05-05T00:04:21.000Z 
Currently the MySQL store plugin tests connectivity to a MySQL server by 
attempting to connect to a 'test' database. 

Not all distributions of MySQL create a 'test' database, connectivity to 
MySQL should be accomplished by some other means.

Comment 1 by WLigtenb...@gmail.com
Aah, this explains.
I got the following error:
Access denied for user 'rdflib'@'localhost' to database 'test'
Even though I never refer to a test database. The user I created was specific for this task, so it only got access to the database it should read and write to/from.
And I also found that Ubuntu 10.10 (Maverick) does not have a test database in the MySQL installation by default.


Comment 2 by gromgull
MySQL support was lost when we made the change to 3.0
It may be moved to rdfextras, see: http://code.google.com/p/rdfextras/issues/detail?id=18


Comment 3 by gromgull
